parsed_target.game_explanation: This 3-player, 3-round chip-trading game features one common-value chip (GREEN at $0.50 for everyone) and two privately valued chips (RED and BLUE). Bear values BLUE and RED quite highly (BLUE=$1.00, RED=$0.60), Buffalo values BLUE moderately (BLUE=$0.70) and RED very little ($0.10), and Goose values both RED and BLUE very low ($0.10 each). All players value GREEN at $0.50. This creates natural directional trade incentives: Goose has relatively low marginal value for colored chips and will often be a willing supplier of BLUE/RED to Bear or Buffalo when they offer GREEN; Bear is strongly motivated to acquire BLUE from either player and will be willing to pay with GREEN or some RED; Buffalo is motivated to trade away RED and perhaps some GREEN to acquire BLUE, but at a slightly lower marginal valuation than Bear. Because non-senders respond simultaneously and the recipient is chosen at random if multiple players accept, proposals that are profitable for multiple potential recipients introduce uncertainty about who the trading partner will be. Senders must balance designing offers that at least one partner finds profitable, while not being so generous that they give away value unnecessarily. Given the personas, all three are not especially punitive and are willing to accept somewhat unequal deals if they are clearly individually profitable. Over three rounds, players are likely to move GREEN chips toward Goose (who uses them to buy value-relevant colored chips for others) and BLUE chips toward Bear and Buffalo. Bear in particular is likely to aggressively propose trades that convert GREEN into BLUE and RED, leveraging Goose’s low private values. Buffalo will look for safe, positive-sum trades that exploit her very low RED value and moderate BLUE value, but she will be cautious about giving up too much GREEN. Goose, who is tolerant of unequal outcomes, will often accept small but positive gains, especially exchanging low-value BLUE/RED for GREEN, and may propose trades to sell off her BLUE/RED if she infers others value them more. However, information remains incomplete: players only know their own valuations, so they must infer others’ preferences from observed acceptances, declines, and the pattern of offers across the three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone has symmetric endowments (10 of each chip) but asymmetric values. No one yet knows the others’ private valuations, so initial offers are likely simple and conservative, designed to test willingness to trade while delivering clear gains for the proposer. We should see early attempts by Buffalo and Bear to use their UI calculators to find obviously profitable one-step trades—most plausibly by offering chips they privately value less (for Buffalo, RED; for Bear, possibly GREEN) in exchange for chips they value more (for both, BLUE). Goose, with low values for RED and BLUE, is more in a position of a supplier of colored chips in return for GREEN, but she does not yet know exactly how much others want those chips. First-round proposals function both as value-extracting moves and as experiments to learn about the others’ willingness to accept certain exchange rates.

By Round 1, some information has been revealed. Buffalo has shown eagerness to trade away RED in order to acquire BLUE, and Goose has demonstrated a willingness to accept modest gains by taking GREEN or RED when those are clearly beneficial. Bear has just pulled BLUE from Buffalo using GREEN, revealing Bear’s strong interest in BLUE and a willingness to sacrifice GREEN. The pattern suggests to all players that BLUE is relatively more valuable to Buffalo and Bear than to Goose, and that GREEN is serving as a common medium of exchange. In this round, players refine strategies: Buffalo and Bear both have fewer RED and GREEN chips and more BLUE relative to the start, while Goose has increased RED holdings and possibly GREEN. Buffalo is likely now more careful with GREEN, preferring targeted BLUE-for-RED trades if possible. Bear will still push to acquire more BLUE or possibly RED from Goose using GREEN, since Bear’s marginal valuation for BLUE remains high. Goose may attempt to sell off some accumulated BLUE or RED for GREEN again, but after a declined attempt at the end of Round 0, she may slightly adjust terms toward more favorable rates for others.

Entering the final round, players know there will be no future opportunities, so they focus on last-chance value extraction rather than information gathering. BLUE chips have increasingly flowed to Bear and Buffalo, while Goose has tended to acquire GREEN and some RED. Everyone now has a clearer qualitative view: Bear strongly chases BLUE and is willing to part with GREEN; Buffalo is happy to part with RED and some GREEN for BLUE but has become more cautious about depleting GREEN too much; Goose is content to convert low-valued BLUE/RED into GREEN whenever profitable. With limited remaining chips and only three proposal opportunities left, each sender tries to identify the highest-surplus trade left that can still be executed given inventories. Generous but still strictly profitable offers are more likely now, because there is no advantage to keeping chips that are low-value to oneself if they can still be traded for higher-value chips. Conflict risk over recipient selection remains if multiple players find the same proposal profitable, but by this point inventory asymmetries mean many offers effectively target a single counterpart who has the right combination of chips.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players value RED the most ($1.00), GREEN at $0.50, and BLUE at different levels: otter $0.40, peacock $0.50, cat $0.70. This makes BLUE the main source of disagreement: BLUE is relatively weak for otter and peacock compared with cat, so efficient trades push BLUE from otter/peacock to cat in exchange for RED (or occasionally GREEN). Since values for RED and GREEN are common across players, there is no private-information gain from trading those colors with each other; gains come from using low-value BLUE to buy higher-value RED or GREEN. However, the rules require proposers to offer a different color from what they request, and recipients privately see their own surplus, so no one will knowingly accept a negative-surplus trade.

Strategically, any trade that increases total dollar value (group surplus) requires BLUE to flow toward cat and RED to flow toward otter/peacock, since cat has the highest BLUE value and everyone values RED equally. For two-player links: otter–cat trades can convert otter’s weaker BLUE into either RED or GREEN that otter values more; cat can profit if she gets enough BLUE relative to the RED/GREEN she gives up. With peacock–cat, they share the same RED value, but cat values BLUE more, so cat benefits from receiving BLUE if she gives up RED or GREEN in the right ratio. With otter–peacock, both value RED at 1.00 and GREEN at 0.50, but peacock’s BLUE is slightly better than otter’s; still, the difference (0.10) is so small that it is hard to find mutually beneficial marginal trades between them without involving cat.

Personality-wise, otter is cooperative and reciprocal but not especially generous in dictator settings; she dislikes uncertainty and is older, so she is likely to propose relatively conservative, clearly self-profitable trades and be cautious about accepting trades that give up many RED chips. Peacock tends to tolerate inequality and has moderate generosity signals but low reciprocity, plus strong uncertainty aversion—she will accept clearly positive trades even if somewhat unequal, but is unlikely to sacrifice her own surplus to help others. Cat is strongly trusting and somewhat prosocial, but still made low ultimatum offers; she may be open to giving others some surplus if she remains clearly better off, especially when she can accumulate BLUE cheaply given her higher BLUE value.

Because recipients see exact dollar gains, most accepted trades will be straightforward win–win offers with clearly positive surplus to both sides; trades that merely reshuffle equal value (e.g., 1 RED for 1 BLUE between cat and peacock) will be declined by the lower-valuing side. Multilateral competition can arise when a proposer posts an obviously profitable trade for more than one responder; then both responders may accept, and the randomization rule creates some risk for the proposer if one counterparty is more favorable than another. But here, since asymmetries are strongest between cat and the others, many offers will be attractive primarily to cat; peacock often faces slimmer margins. Over the three rounds, players start with symmetric holdings (10 of each color) and limited time; they are likely to focus on simple, small trades that incrementally convert BLUE to RED for otter/peacock and RED (or some GREEN) to BLUE for cat, with some failed attempts when proposers misjudge how much surplus the others require to accept.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, everyone begins with 10 of each color and only knows their own chip values. They must infer others’ values from what is proposed or accepted. Otter moves first and, being cooperative but cautious, is likely to propose a conservative trade that clearly benefits herself without being obviously exploitative—probably trying to offload some BLUE for RED or GREEN. Peacock then acts, having seen otter’s outcome, and may probe cat’s BLUE appetite with a modest RED-for-BLUE style trade. Cat moves last and, as a high-BLUE valuer with a trusting/prosocial lean, is likely to seek RED while parting with BLUE in a way that still leaves her better off, especially if earlier behavior signals that the others value BLUE less.

By the start of round 1, cat has revealed that she’s willing to accept BLUE-for-RED at a 3:2 ratio, implying a relatively high BLUE valuation. Otter gains confidence that trading away BLUE is fruitful; peacock learns that trades giving cat BLUE for RED can be mutually beneficial but must be carefully priced. Otter, going first again, is likely to propose another BLUE-for-RED trade, perhaps slightly richer for the recipient to encourage competition or continued acceptance. Peacock then may attempt a more balanced trade with cat that still moves BLUE to cat and RED back to herself. Cat, having one successful trade with otter already and one failed attempt of her own, is likely to refine her ask to something that is clearly positive for at least one of the other two, probably offering BLUE for RED at a slightly more generous ratio than before.

Entering the final round, BLUE has shifted significantly from otter and peacock to cat, while RED has flowed in the opposite direction. Everyone now has clearer beliefs: cat’s willingness to trade substantial BLUE for RED has been demonstrated, and both otter and peacock have executed at least one profitable trade with her. With limited time left, proposers prioritize clearly positive, simple trades. Peacock moves first and may try a last BLUE-for-RED conversion with cat, though margins are tight. Otter, second, has relatively low BLUE left and high RED, so she might now target GREEN if she sees it as less crucial, or she may attempt one more small BLUE-for-RED deal if feasible. Cat, moving last in the whole game, is likely to post a trade that is straightforwardly beneficial to at least one counterpart, probably offering BLUE and asking for RED at a ratio that guarantees them a positive gain, maximizing the chance of one final accepted exchange.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start symmetric in chip counts (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but with different private values for RED and BLUE; GREEN is common-value at $0.50 each. Badger values both RED and BLUE highly ($0.80 each), camel values RED moderately ($0.60) and BLUE weakly ($0.40), and squid values BLUE moderately ($0.60) but RED poorly ($0.20). This creates strong gains-from-trade opportunities: squid is naturally willing to give up RED (low value) for BLUE (higher value) or GREEN; camel prefers RED to BLUE and is comfortable taking unequal splits; badger likes both RED and BLUE but is numerate and cooperative, likely to seek trades that are modestly favorable yet acceptable to others. Because all players see their own surplus from any proposed trade, they will not knowingly accept negative-surplus deals, and proposals that are too one-sided will simply be declined. However, proposers do not know others’ exact chip values, so early in the game they must infer preferences from acceptance behavior (e.g., if squid accepts trades that exchange away RED, this reveals RED is relatively low value to squid). Three-player uncertainty is amplified by the rule that if more than one responder accepts, the counterparty is chosen randomly: this makes it risky to design a proposal targeting one specific partner if it would be bad to execute with the other. Reasonable proposers will therefore look for structures that are non-negative for both potential recipients, mildly generous to increase acceptance, but tilted in their own favor. Given the personas, camel and squid are relatively tolerant of unequal outcomes and willing to accept small positive gains; badger is cooperative but not strongly altruistic. Over three rounds with nine total proposal turns, we expect experimentation in early turns (small trades in RED/BLUE to probe others’ directions of preference) and then more targeted, surplus-increasing trades once patterns of acceptance emerge. Because GREEN is equally valued, pure swaps of GREEN for another chip are zero-sum for value and only useful instrumentally (e.g., to bundle or re-balance). Most efficiency gains will come from moving RED away from squid toward camel and badger, and moving BLUE away from camel toward badger and squid. The following forecast instantiates one plausible full play-through where players gradually discover these complementarities, with some declined proposals early and more accepted trades once they infer each other's preferences.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, no one knows the others’ private RED/BLUE valuations. Rational, numerate players will likely start with small, low-risk trades that generate a clear positive surplus for themselves and seem plausibly acceptable to at least one counterpart. Because GREEN is common-value, using GREEN as a medium of exchange is safe: no one strictly loses value from swapping GREEN in balanced quantities, so modest imbalances can be used to tilt surplus. Camel’s persona suggests willingness to accept modest gains; squid is prosocial and tolerant as well. Badger, moving first, is likely to probe BLUE demand or RED supply. Initial declinations will help each player infer which directions (RED-for-BLUE, BLUE-for-RED, or involving GREEN) are attractive to others. By the end of round 0, we expect at least one successful trade revealing that squid is happy to shed RED and camel likes RED more than BLUE.

After round 0, some structure is revealed. Camel has successfully traded 3 BLUE for 2 RED with squid, suggesting to observers that camel values RED more than BLUE, and squid is willing to give up RED for BLUE. Squid then traded 4 RED for 2 BLUE with badger, revealing squid has low RED value and decent BLUE value, while badger is comfortable parting with BLUE to gain RED at a favorable rate. Inventories after round 0 are skewed: badger holds more RED and fewer BLUE, camel holds more RED and fewer BLUE, and squid holds fewer RED and more BLUE. Given this, badger now expects that squid will continue to accept trades that offload RED; camel expects that both others like RED; and squid anticipates that offers involving giving up RED will be attractive to others. Players can now design more targeted proposals that create positive surplus for both sides based on these inferred preferences, while remaining cautious about the random-recipient rule if both responders might accept.

By round 2, the history has clarified underlying values: squid consistently offers RED and asks for BLUE, confirming RED is his lowest-value chip; camel has shown willingness to give up BLUE for RED and some reluctance to trade GREEN; badger has traded to acquire RED and is selective with BLUE but will accept surplus-positive deals. Inventories are now tilted: badger and camel are relatively RED-rich and BLUE-poor compared to squid. With only one round left, players recognize limited time to exploit remaining gains from trade. Badger can still profitably exchange some RED for BLUE with squid if the rate is favorable to both; camel might look for a final small gain but risks declinations if offers are too lopsided; squid, having already improved his position, may accept any remaining trade that is clearly positive surplus but avoid trades that give up too much BLUE for RED. Strategic uncertainty remains due to the random-recipient rule, but given past responses, proposers can target offers likely to be accepted by exactly one counterpart, reducing randomness and lock in final marginal improvements.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips and common values for green chips. Every player starts with 10 chips of each color and only knows their own red/blue valuations, but all know that green is always worth $0.50 to everyone. This structure makes green a natural numéraire and safe medium of exchange: trading greens away is cognitively salient as giving up guaranteed $0.50 per chip. The key hidden asymmetry is butterfly’s strong preference for BLUE ($0.90) versus others’ low blue values (dog $0.20, ram $0.10), and dog’s strong preference for RED ($1.00) versus butterfly ($0.60) and ram ($0.40). Ram is weak on both red and blue (especially blue) and values green the same as others. Surplus-creating trades exist when high-value specialists exchange with low-value holders: dog should want to acquire red from butterfly/ram; butterfly should want to acquire blue from dog/ram; ram has modest gains moving blue to butterfly or red to dog when compensated with green or the opposite color. However, the proposer doesn’t know others’ valuations and must rely on simple markups that still look profitable in their own calculator while enticing at least one counterpart to accept. Because acceptance decisions are private and simultaneous, senders face uncertainty not only over whether any given player accepts but also over which accepting player will be matched if more than one says yes. This pushes senders toward offers that are safe for them even in the least-favorable recipient case, often limiting generosity. Personality priors suggest all three are relatively tolerant of unequal splits and not strong punishers of mild unfairness; they are willing to take small personal gains without demanding strict equity. Butterfly and ram look somewhat self-protective in dictator tasks but not aggressively so, while dog is a cooperative, closure-seeking type who dislikes prolonged uncertainty. Together, these traits make it likely that modest, clearly-positive offers will be accepted, especially when they exchange clearly low-value-for-high-value chips from each player’s perspective. Over the three rounds, dog will tend to probe with low-risk red-for-blue or red-for-green trades that keep her value non-negative; butterfly will aggressively arbitrage blue (selling low-value chips for him and buying high-value blue or at least red), and ram will be cautious but opportunistic, accepting deals that produce even small positive gains in his calculator. As proposals accumulate and holdings diverge, each player still only sees movements of counts, not values, so the dynamic is that of groping toward mutually beneficial patterns without ever fully inferring others’ types. The limited three-round horizon and fixed proposer order reduce incentives for long-run reputation or punishment; players focus on immediate surplus, and by late rounds may accept even small positive deals rather than risk ending with unused surplus opportunities.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, all players hold symmetric inventories (10 of each chip) but asymmetric and unknown valuations. Strategically, this is the purest exploration round: nobody has seen any revealed preference yet, so proposals will be simple and low-risk. Dog, valuing red highly and blue weakly, is likely to try trading away blue or green to pick up extra red, while ensuring her surplus calculator never shows a loss. Butterfly, a high-blue type, will probe by trying to acquire blue or shed low-value red against something that might tempt others. Ram, who values both red and blue relatively low and green at par, will try to capture incremental gains, probably by selling his particularly low-valued blue to whoever bites. Acceptance behavior will be conservative but not strict: given their relatively low ultimatum thresholds and low rejection tendencies, all three are inclined to accept clearly positive, even if uneven, trades. With no prior history, offers will cluster around small, easily-evaluated bundles (1–3 chips) that clearly show green or red/blue improvements on each proposer’s screen.

After round 0, some information is indirectly revealed. Dog has shown a willingness to give up multiple blue chips for a single red, hinting that red might be relatively valuable to her or blue relatively cheap. Butterfly accepted a 2 BLUE for 1 RED trade and later willingly traded away red for blue, which from dog and ram’s perspective suggests that butterfly might value blue quite highly relative to red. Ram’s acceptance of a 3 RED for 3 BLUE trade signals that he doesn’t strongly distinguish between red and blue or at least found the swap close enough to neutral. Ram’s later sale of 4 BLUE for 2 GREEN indicates that his blue is notably low-value to him compared to certain $0.50 greens. All three now see that butterfly is an eager buyer of blue and that ram is willing to dump blue for green; dog appears to be a red specialist. In round 1, dog can exploit this by trying to buy more red relatively cheaply from ram or butterfly using her low-value blue; butterfly can further arbitrage by acquiring low-value blue from ram and maybe even from dog if prices are attractive; ram may continue to reposition toward green while watching for any small yet transparent surplus. Offers may become slightly more aggressive as each player tests how far the others’ willingness to trade extends, but they still face uncertainty over exact valuations and have to keep each proposed trade non-negative in their own calculator.

By the final round, players have seen a pattern: butterfly eagerly accumulates blue, ram repeatedly sells blue for green and is at least moderately willing to trade red, and dog consistently uses blue to buy red. They still do not know each other’s exact values but have directional beliefs: dog is a red buyer, butterfly a blue buyer, ram a low-blue type who prefers green and is flexible on red. The finite horizon now matters: there are only three proposals left, so each player becomes more willing to accept even modest gains, since unused opportunities will disappear. Dog, leading off again, has an incentive to secure any remaining advantageous red acquisitions using blue if she still has excess low-value blue; butterfly will look to convert remaining low-value holdings (especially red or even some green) into high-value blue if possible; ram, with limited remaining chances, will take conservative, calculator-positive offers rather than gamble on better future trades. Proposals may be slightly more aggressive in terms of spreads, but repeated interaction and the observed acceptance patterns push them toward trades that remain safely positive for at least one counterpart. Declined offers can still arise if a proposer overestimates how cheap others consider red or green, but overall, the final round tends toward closing out obvious arbitrage opportunities rather than experimentation.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 of each chip, but value red and blue very differently while green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Iguana values red highly and blue weakly (R=0.80, B=0.40), Lion values blue highly and red moderately (B=1.00, R=0.40), and Alligator has intermediate valuations (B=0.60, R=0.30). This creates strong gains from trade in red–blue swaps: Iguana is a natural red buyer / blue seller, Lion is a blue buyer / red seller, and Alligator sits in between, able to profit from trading with either side. Because green is valued equally by everyone, it serves mainly as a neutral sweetener or safe medium for early low-stakes tests, not a source of pure surplus. The primary uncertainty is that each player knows only their own values, so they must infer the others’ preferences from which trades are offered and accepted. Competition and randomness also matter: if two recipients accept, the partner is chosen at random, so proposals that are attractive to multiple players are riskier for the sender. Given the personas, Lion is relatively trusting and willing to experiment with mutually beneficial trades; Iguana is tolerant of unequal outcomes and unlikely to reject small but positive gains; Alligator is cautious but numerate and dislikes uncertainty, preferring clearly positive, modest trades. Over three rounds (nine total proposal turns), we should see early low-risk exploratory trades, emerging red–blue specialization where Lion and Iguana move toward their preferred colors, and Alligator using information from prior turns to propose conservative but profitable deals, especially once others’ revealed preferences become clearer.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has symmetric chip holdings but asymmetric and unknown valuations. Round 0 is mainly about discovery: players will test simple trades to probe others’ willingness to part with specific colors. Given the risk of multiple acceptances and Alligator’s uncertainty aversion, early proposals are likely to involve green—safe, common-value chips—as a way to experiment without risking large valuation losses. Once someone reveals a willingness to trade red for blue or vice versa, others can infer skewed values and start moving toward more targeted red–blue swaps.

By the start of Round 1, Lion and Iguana have just completed a small red-for-green trade that left Iguana slightly better off and Lion slightly worse off, a plausible outcome for a trusting Lion making an exploratory offer. All players now see that Iguana is willing to give up some green for red and did not strongly resist a slightly unfavorable deal, hinting that red may be particularly valuable to Iguana. Alligator’s first proposal was universally declined, signaling that exchanging red for green at that rate was not attractive to others. With these weak signals, players begin shifting toward red–blue trades that better exploit comparative advantages, though they still avoid very large, risky offers.

Going into the final round, revealed behavior shows clear patterns: Lion repeatedly accepts chances to trade away red for blue at favorable or roughly break-even terms; Iguana has accepted trades that increase red and later has been willing to send blue for red, reinforcing that Iguana values red highly and blue weakly; Alligator has made a cautious but profitable red-for-blue proposal that Lion accepted. With only one proposal left per player, they now focus on locking in any remaining profitable swaps. Lion and Iguana are natural partners for large red–blue trades, while Alligator looks for a last small improvement without risking a loss. Risk of random partner selection is now lower because proposals are structured to be attractive primarily to one counterparty.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player, three-round bargaining game where each player starts with an identical endowment of 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips, but values RED and BLUE differently; GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone. That asymmetry makes certain bilateral trades strictly value-creating: RED is especially valuable to whale-red-86 and duck-orange-4971 ($1.00) while only moderately valuable to dolphin-orange-3122 ($0.60). BLUE is especially valuable to dolphin-orange-3122 ($0.80), moderately valuable to duck-orange-4971 ($0.60), and relatively low-value to whale-red-86 ($0.40). Thus, efficient trades move RED toward whale and duck and move BLUE toward dolphin and duck, while GREEN mainly serves as a neutral medium or balancing chip because its value is common and flat. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own private values; they see that others may value chips somewhere in the public range but must infer who especially wants RED or BLUE from observed trades. Strategic tension comes from the rule that if both non-senders accept a proposal, the recipient is chosen at random, so proposers must consider that any obviously favorable-to-others deal might attract multiple acceptances. All three personas are relatively tolerant of unequal outcomes and not strongly punitive, so we expect mostly self-interested but non-hostile bargaining: players make clearly positive-surplus offers for the partner side, with modest margins for themselves, especially early on. Over the three rounds, proposers will likely test simple, low-complexity trades that use GREEN as a neutral chip and create value by reallocating high-value colors toward themselves. Because everyone sees realized trades and evolving inventories, later rounds can feature slightly sharper, more targeted proposals as players infer who is willing to part with which colors. However, limited rounds and short time horizons mean players are unlikely to hold out for perfect deals; instead they will accept any trade that gives them a noticeable positive gain without creating a big perceived loss of flexibility for future turns.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold symmetric inventories and have no information about others' private values, beyond public ranges. Proposers will likely test simple, small trades to probe others' willingness to exchange RED and BLUE, often using GREEN as the payment medium because it is common-value and psychologically neutral. Given the personas, we expect them to avoid obviously negative offers for the other side; both responders are willing to accept small but clear gains, so early trades that give $0.10–$0.30 to a responder are likely to be accepted. This round is mostly about discovering which players are relatively more keen on moving RED versus BLUE.

After round 0, the history shows that dolphin-orange-3122 was willing to buy BLUE with GREEN and trade away RED for BLUE, revealing an especially high value for BLUE and lower value for RED. Duck-orange-4971 readily traded GREEN for RED, revealing a strong preference for RED and comfort using GREEN as currency. Whale-red-86 traded away low-value BLUE for GREEN and then bought RED with GREEN, revealing high value for RED and low value for BLUE. By round 1, each player has strong evidence that dolphin values BLUE highly, whale and duck both value RED highly, and GREEN is widely used as money. Strategic focus now shifts to refining positions: dolphin wants more BLUE and can sell RED or maybe GREEN; whale wants more RED and doesn’t mind holding GREEN; duck wants more RED but also likes BLUE moderately. With limited rounds, they aim for clear win–win trades without over-negotiating.

Entering the final round, players have already executed several mutually beneficial trades and have a clear sense of each others' preferences: dolphin-orange-3122 strongly pursues BLUE and gives up RED and GREEN; whale-red-86 aggressively acquires RED while shedding BLUE; duck-orange-4971 also chases RED but is flexible with BLUE and GREEN. Inventories are now skewed: whale is heavy in RED and GREEN and very low in BLUE; dolphin is heavy in BLUE and lighter in RED and GREEN; duck is RED-heavy with moderate BLUE and lower GREEN. With only one proposal left for each player, the priority is to grab any remaining positive-sum opportunities, usually modest adjustments rather than large swings. Because everyone has revealed a willingness to accept small but clear gains, final trades are likely to be straightforward and thin-margin but still strictly beneficial to both sides, avoiding complicated or risky offers that might be rejected in the last chance.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color, and can only trade by proposing a one-color-for-another exchange. The key driver of surplus is the heterogeneity in red and blue valuations: gorilla values red most highly ($1.00) and blue moderately ($0.70); turtle values blue most highly ($1.00) and red slightly less ($0.90); zebra values both red ($0.60) and blue ($0.50) relatively low compared to the others. Green is neutral for everyone ($0.50), so it is flexible but never creates gains from trade on its own. Efficient trades tend to move red chips toward gorilla (who values them most) and blue chips toward turtle (who values them most), with zebra acting as a relatively low-valuation intermediary willing to part with red or blue for modest gains if offered favorable green or cross-color terms. Strategic uncertainty arises because each player only sees their own valuations and must infer others’ preferences from proposals and acceptances; they know that blue and red are somewhere between $0.10 and $1.00 for others, but not exactly where. The UI surplus calculator removes cognitive barriers to evaluating any given trade and makes it unlikely that players will knowingly accept strictly negative deals in money terms, but they may still accept very small gains due to prosocial tendencies or low fairness thresholds. Turtle is moderately prosocial, numerate, and somewhat uncertainty-averse; she will seek clearly positive surplus trades and is unlikely to give much away for free. Gorilla is tolerant of unequal outcomes and quite willing to accept small but positive gains; she is also strongly motivated to accumulate red chips and may be flexible in what she offers for them. Zebra is highly prosocial and tolerant of unequal splits, and might accept trades that give her even small gains while delivering larger advantages to others, especially if they appear fair or cooperative. Across three rounds, each player proposes once per round in a fixed order, and trades update holdings immediately. Early moves are likely exploratory, probing others’ willingness to exchange blue for red or green for red/blue at modest markups. As the game proceeds, players learn that gorilla strongly pursues red, turtle strongly pursues blue, and zebra is relatively easygoing and willing to accommodate. Due to the short, three-round structure and lack of future penalties, players will mostly focus on straightforward, immediately profitable trades rather than intricate multi-step strategies, but fairness and cooperation cues will still shape whether marginally profitable offers to others are actually accepted.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip color and only know their own valuations. Turtle, who values blue highly, suspects that some players may value red more and will likely test a simple blue-for-red trade that is clearly profitable for her but not blatantly unfair. Gorilla knows she values red strongly and will aim to acquire red, probably offering blue or green in a way that stays profitable but attractive enough for at least one other player given her own uncertainty about their values. Zebra, highly prosocial and value-tolerant, is likely to propose an offer that looks quite fair in quantity terms, perhaps even slightly generous to partners, to ensure at least one trade executes. This first round is exploratory: each player is feeling out what others might be willing to accept and who seems eager to specialize in which colors.

After round 0, public history shows that gorilla successfully traded away blue for red with zebra, signaling that gorilla strongly desires red and that zebra is willing to move red at modest terms. Turtle saw her initial even red-for-blue proposal fail, suggesting that at least one other player also values blue fairly highly or dislikes even swaps. Gorilla now has 13 red, 6 blue, and 10 green; zebra has 7 red, 14 blue, and 10 green; turtle remains at the initial 10/10/10. Turtle has learned that zebra is a relatively flexible counterparty and that gorilla wants red. Zebra, having gained blue and lost red, might try to rebalance by offering blue for red again, but her strong prosocial orientation makes her willing to propose trades that are not strongly in her favor if they seem cooperative; at the same time, others’ revealed behavior will constrain what is actually accepted.

Heading into the final round, holdings reflect the two successful trades so far. Turtle now has 12 red, 7 blue, and 10 green, having profitably exchanged blue for red with zebra; gorilla sits on 13 red, 6 blue, and 10 green; zebra has 5 red, 17 blue, and 10 green, having twice traded away red for blue. With only one proposal left for each player, there is no need to preserve reputation for future play, so choices focus purely on immediate surplus and any intrinsic fairness or prosocial motives. Turtle, having achieved a strong red position, may attempt a small additional gain by offering green for blue, but others can compute that green and blue are equal or higher value for them, making acceptance unlikely unless the deal is clearly neutral or better for them. Gorilla might try to flip some of her high-value red to turtle in exchange for blue, but since both value those colors highly, finding mutually beneficial terms is difficult without revealing private values, so she may instead refrain from risky offers and propose a conservative green-for-blue trade that others nonetheless expect to be unappealing. Zebra, already heavily tilted toward blue and low on red, might want more red, but both turtle and gorilla know they especially like red, so they will tend not to accept losing trades; zebra’s final offer is likely to be somewhat prosocial but still non-negative for herself, and it may fail if it does not create enough private surplus for others.

This means efficient trade tends to move RED toward cat and iguana and BLUE toward zebra. Since no one knows others’ exact values, they infer preferences from observed acceptances and rejections. All three personas are relatively tolerant of unequal outcomes and not strongly punitive, so we expect mostly self-interested but not spiteful behavior and little rejection based purely on fairness. As repeated proposers in a short 3-round game with 3 turns per round, players balance extracting surplus with the risk of getting nothing if they propose too aggressively. Random resolution when multiple recipients accept creates uncertainty: a proposer cannot target a specific partner and must consider how both potential recipients will respond. Over the game, we expect: (1) early feeling-out trades where proposers test modestly favorable rates; (2) a pattern where cat and iguana try to shed BLUE to zebra and acquire RED, while zebra tries to swap RED for BLUE; (3) increasing willingness to transact in later rounds as time pressure rises and remaining surplus opportunities shrink. Because all are rational with good numeracy (especially cat and iguana), we forecast mostly individually positive-surplus trades with some modest win–win structures rather than highly exploitative or dominated deals.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, each player holds 10 of each chip. No one has yet observed others’ behavior, so beliefs about others’ valuations are diffuse. Proposers will likely start with relatively simple, small-size trades that are clearly profitable for themselves and plausibly acceptable to at least one partner. Cat and iguana both want more RED and are wary of overpaying with BLUE or GREEN, while zebra is keen to acquire BLUE and is relatively willing to part with RED. Because nobody knows others’ exact values, trades will not be perfectly efficient at first; proposals will serve partly as information-gathering about what others are willing to accept.

After round 0, everyone has seen that zebra is willing to exchange RED for BLUE at roughly 1:1 or slightly better for the counterparty, and that iguana’s attempt to swap RED for BLUE at parity was unattractive to others. Cat has already profited from trades with zebra and now holds more RED and fewer BLUE, reinforcing cat’s incentive to keep acquiring RED if the price is good. Iguana has learned that others do not want to give up BLUE cheaply, and may pivot to using GREEN or smaller imbalances in rates to entice acceptance. Zebra, having seen active demand for BLUE, will try to continue trading RED for BLUE but perhaps in slightly less generous terms or directed toward iguana rather than cat if cat’s BLUE inventory is lower.

By the final round, players have clearer signals: zebra aggressively wants BLUE and will often part with RED; cat is happy to gain RED even in small quantities when the price is near or below $0.50 per chip given GREEN’s benchmark; iguana values RED but has shown willingness to exploit good GREEN-for-BLUE or RED-for-BLUE terms. With only three turns left, proposers become more willing to post clearly positive-surplus offers that are somewhat generous to ensure acceptance rather than risk ending with unused gains from trade. Each player also considers their current inventory: cat is relatively RED-heavy and BLUE-light, iguana has rebalanced toward more BLUE from zebra, and zebra has shed substantial RED while accumulating BLUE and some GREEN. The final trades are likely to be small, tactical improvements in portfolio composition rather than large structural shifts.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. All players start with 10 of each color; green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so green trades are pure transfers with no efficiency gain. The only potential surplus comes from moving red chips from swan (who values red at $0.50) to bear or peacock (who each value red at $0.80), and possibly moving blue chips from peacock ($0.70) to bear or swan ($0.80). Because each player only sees their own valuations, they do not know who has comparative advantages, so they must infer from others’ behavior whether a given color is widely valued or not. All three personas lean self-protective and tolerant of inequality, suggesting they will accept any strictly positive surplus offers and propose trades that give others very little gain if possible. The main friction is uncertainty: each proposer wants to design offers that are attractive enough to be accepted without knowing the recipient’s exact values, while also facing the constraint that if both potential recipients accept, a partner is chosen at random. Over three short rounds with one proposal per player per round, the players will likely focus on straightforward one-color-for-one-color trades that are obviously profitable for themselves and at least weakly plausible for others, gradually revealing which chip types different players seem willing to give up or demand.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has symmetric holdings but different private values. No one yet has information from observed trades. Bear and peacock both highly value red and moderately to highly value blue, while swan values blue highly but red relatively low. Rationally, the biggest surplus is from moving red away from swan toward bear or peacock, and blue away from peacock toward bear or swan. However, with no prior signals, proposers will likely try simple, small-volume trades that are sure gains for themselves and plausibly attractive to at least one partner, without giving away green (which is obviously fair and thus less informative). Round 0 is exploratory: offers will be modest in size and focused on trying to discover others’ willingness to trade red versus blue.

After round 0, no green-for-blue trades went through, suggesting both peacock and swan value blue or at least do not want to trade it for green at 1:1. The successful trade was swan giving blue for red, which made bear better off and slightly hurt swan, meaning swan likely misjudged relative values or was testing the waters with a symmetric-looking swap. Observationally, bear now has more blue and less red; swan holds fewer blue and more red. Bear has also learned that at least one partner is willing to trade blue for red at 1:1, reinforcing bear’s belief that red may be attractive to others too, or that blue may be relatively cheap for some. In round 1, proposers will respond: bear may now try to offload some red toward swan or peacock while collecting more blue, and peacock might cautiously test swapping red or blue, while swan will likely be more careful, preferring trades that show a clear personal gain on the calculator.

By the start of round 2, everyone has now seen multiple swap proposals between red and blue at 1:1 and some acceptances. Bear has repeatedly accepted trades that give more blue for red, indicating to observant players that bear values blue at least as much as red. Swan has both given blue for red and then later red for blue, likely realizing that blue is personally more valuable than red. Peacock, whose blue is slightly less valuable privately than red but still high, has seen that both others are willing to engage in red–blue swaps near parity. With only one round left, players focus on locking in any last strictly positive-surplus trades they can identify. Given the revealed pattern, the most profitable deals now are for bear and swan to continue exchanging red and blue in ways that align with their true valuations—bear and swan both strongly value blue, but bear also values red highly, while swan sees more marginal gain from blue. Peacock, relatively cautious and resource-protective, is less likely to engage unless a trade is clearly beneficial.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red and blue chips and common-value green chips, with each player starting from an equal 10–10–10 inventory. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so trading green mainly serves to rebalance red and blue holdings, not to change total surplus. The strategic friction comes from the fact that red and blue have different private values: monkey values blue very highly ($0.90) and red low ($0.20); camel values both blue and red moderately high ($0.80 and $0.50); gorilla is symmetric and lowish on both red and blue ($0.30 and $0.30). None of them see others’ valuations, but all see the trade history and know that red/blue prices are heterogeneous within a $0.10–$1 band, which creates uncertainty about where surplus-sharing opportunities lie.

The main surplus possibilities come from moving blue chips toward monkey (the highest blue valuer) and, secondarily, from moving red chips toward camel (who values red more than gorilla and monkey). Gorilla, who dislikes uncertainty and is relatively tolerant of small payoffs, is likely to propose modest, conservative trades that visibly benefit her on the calculator without being extreme, hoping that at least one counterpart sees a small positive gain. Camel, with a very self-protective social profile and strong reasoning skills, is likely to offer trades that are quite favorable to himself and only weakly positive or even slightly negative to others—he will expect the calculator to enforce discipline and may rely on at least one counterpart misjudging others’ valuations or being mildly prosocial. Monkey is more prosocial and trusting, so he may be willing to accept small personal gains or even break-even trades early on, especially if they appear roughly fair, in order to reposition chips for later advantage.

The random recipient rule under multiple acceptances creates strategic ambiguity: proposers cannot target a specific partner, so both the structure and sizes of offers must be chosen to be acceptable to at least one plausible counterpart while not being too attractive to both in ways that might change the expected surplus split. At the same time, because there are only three rounds and each player proposes three times total, there is limited scope for long-run reputation. Players will each largely focus on immediate calculator gains, with modest expectations of follow-up trades.

Given the valuations, we can expect many proposals involving red-for-blue or red-for-green swaps. Gorilla will tend to trade away blue or red if she can pick up green (which is the same value but psychologically safer, since it has no hidden heterogeneity). Camel will seek to accumulate red and blue while being reluctant to give up green unless the proposal is very favorable. Monkey will strongly prefer to accumulate blue and may be relatively happy to offload red, especially if he can do so in ways that do not strongly exploit others. Over the three rounds, expect a small number of accepted trades that move blue chips to monkey and red chips to camel, with gorilla often serving as a flexible counterpart in at least one trade, given her lower thresholds and preference for predictable, calculator-confirmed gains. A fair number of proposals will likely be declined when they are obviously one-sided on the calculator for the candidate recipient.

The following forecast reconstructs one internally consistent, plausible sequence of proposals and responses that respects the proposer schedule, inventory constraints, and each player’s priors, while using the surplus calculator logic to ensure that any accepted trade is individually beneficial (or, for monkey, at least not clearly harmful) from the recipient’s own perspective.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each color. No one has seen any trades yet, so their beliefs about others’ valuations are diffuse. Gorilla proposes first and, given her preference for predictability and moderate social orientation, will likely try a small, symmetric-looking trade that improves her position in terms of her own calculator while not appearing extreme. Camel, who follows, has not yet observed acceptance behavior and may test whether he can cheaply acquire red or blue without sacrificing too much green. Monkey, moving last in the round, will observe two failed or successful offers and may reveal his strong preference for blue by his proposal choice. Overall, round 0 is exploratory, with modest proposals as players feel out each other’s willingness to trade and the practical constraints of the random-recipient rule.

Going into round 1, gorilla’s exploratory trade was rejected, as was camel’s, but monkey’s offer to buy blue with red was accepted by camel. Publicly, everyone sees that camel just accepted a trade where he gave up 2 blue for 3 red. This suggests to others that camel may put relatively high value on red compared to blue, and that monkey is eager to accumulate blue. Gorilla infers that very minor, nearly break-even trades are not attractive when they involve sacrificing green, and will likely adjust by offering a trade where she gains green or blue more clearly. Camel now knows that monkey likes blue and is willing to trade away red; he may try to exploit this by offering deals that look just slightly positive for monkey but strongly favorable to himself. Monkey, having successfully improved his blue position, is willing to consider additional trades that further align his portfolio toward blue, but he will also be wary of giving up too much green, which he knows is valuable to everyone.

By round 2, the pattern is clearer. Camel has twice accepted trades that give him more red while sacrificing blue or green, signaling that he values red strongly and is willing to trade away some common-value green when the calculator shows a good gain. Gorilla has now accepted once, trading red for green when the numbers were favorable, showing that she is pragmatic and responsive to even modest expected gains. Monkey has executed one successful trade to gain blue and has declined others that require giving up green without sufficient blue compensation. Each player now has some updated beliefs: camel is a hard bargainer but opportunistic, gorilla is conservative but flexible, and monkey is clearly blue-hungry but not reckless with green.

With only one round left, camel, proposing first, will try to secure a final favorable deal that further increases his high-value red holdings or recovers some green, targeting monkey’s thirst for blue. Gorilla, going second, will look for a clean, low-risk final adjustment, likely again involving red-for-green if she can induce acceptance. Monkey, last to propose in the game, faces the final opportunity to move chips and will prioritize a trade that secures additional blue for red or green if a counterpart is still willing. However, after several declined offers involving green, both gorilla and camel are now wary of giving up green, so monkey may struggle to craft a proposal that both gives him significant blue and yields others a visible net gain in their calculators.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with symmetric chip inventories (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but hold different private values for RED and BLUE, while GREEN is common-value at $0.50. Alligator values RED the most ($0.70), then GREEN ($0.50), then BLUE ($0.30). Otter values BLUE the most ($0.50), then GREEN ($0.50), then RED ($0.30). Tiger values RED highly ($0.60), then GREEN ($0.50), and BLUE the least ($0.20). Efficient gains from trade arise whenever a player with relatively low value for a color can exchange it with someone who values it more, particularly trading BLUE from tiger to otter or alligator, and trading RED from otter to alligator or tiger. However, players only know their own values and the public value range ($0.10–$1.00) for RED/BLUE, so they must infer others’ valuations from observed acceptances and rejections over time. The surplus calculator ensures that every accept or reject decision is made with full awareness of each player’s own net gain or loss, so dominated trades (strict personal loss) are extremely unlikely. Persona priors suggest that alligator is relatively prosocial and tolerant of modest personal disadvantage but still value-sensitive; otter is strongly self-oriented in allocations yet tolerant of receiving little, likely to accept even small personal gains; tiger is prosocial and strategically thoughtful, more willing to propose equitable or generous trades if they still yield some gain. Across three short rounds with an exogenous order, proposers face uncertainty about which counterpart values what, so early offers are likely to be relatively conservative and simple (small quantities, minimal surplus) and may fail. Accepted trades will typically be slightly favorable to the proposer but still weakly beneficial to the recipient, with more aggressive and targeted proposals emerging once a few accept/reject patterns reveal who likely values RED or BLUE more.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, no one yet has information about others’ private values for RED and BLUE, beyond the shared range. Proposers are likely to start with small, low-risk trades that are personally profitable according to their own calculator but modest enough that at least one recipient will also see a weak gain. Because GREEN is common-value, it will often serve as a neutral medium in early trades when someone wants to test how others react to RED or BLUE requests. Rejections here are mostly about mismatched valuations rather than strategic punishment.

By the second round, some information has emerged. Alligator and otter successfully traded RED for BLUE, revealing to each that the other also finds the swap at least weakly favorable. Tiger saw that otter refused to buy RED for BLUE at 1:1 and that alligator accepted RED for BLUE from tiger at a 2-for-3 ratio, signaling that alligator values RED more than BLUE and that otter may not value RED much. Players now adjust: alligator and tiger each see the other as a natural RED-intensive partner, while otter is positioned as a BLUE-intensive holder. Proposals become slightly more targeted, trying to exploit these inferred comparative advantages while still staying simple and small enough to avoid clear losses for any responder.

Entering the final round, trade history has clarified the likely valuation pattern: otter appears to value BLUE highly and RED weakly (willing to give up several RED for BLUE), whereas alligator and tiger consistently move in the direction of holding more RED and shedding BLUE where possible. Both alligator and tiger have exploited favorable RED-for-BLUE ratios already, so remaining proposals are more marginal and cautious: inventories are less symmetric, and easy high-surplus trades are mostly exhausted. Players may either attempt one last small, nearly risk-free improvement or decline trades that do not clearly help them, especially if they suspect they have already reached a near-efficient allocation given their beliefs.

rounds.round_explanation: Players begin with no history and only their own values. Dolphin, who dislikes uncertainty and is numerate, is likely to start with a small, safe probe that sheds low-value RED to see if either partner is keen on red. Frog, who highly values RED and is quite prosocial, is inclined to make offers that are fairly generous but still strongly move her toward more RED. Pig is tolerant of unequal outcomes and somewhat impulsive, so his early choices will lean toward any trade that yields a clear, immediate gain, especially in BLUE or RED, without much subtle inference about others’ types yet.

After round 0, everyone has learned something. Dolphin just made a small profit trading into BLUE, then a huge profit trading BLUE for RED, revealing she is surprisingly eager to move into RED at generous terms; this strongly signals that RED might be cheap for her and that BLUE may not be her only priority. Frog’s willingness to pay many BLUE for RED at even terms for dolphin hints that frog’s valuation of RED is high and of BLUE is low. Pig has seen profitable opportunities slip by and a declined green-for-blue attempt, learning that neither partner is desperate for GREEN at a bad rate. In round 1, dolphin can refine her strategy and exploit frog’s RED preference, frog can try to correct her earlier overgenerosity while still chasing RED, and pig may search for safer, more balanced deals, especially involving BLUE where his value is higher than frog’s.

By the final round, patterns are clearer. Frog’s repeated moves toward RED expose that she values red chips much more than blue or green. Dolphin has revealed that she values BLUE highly but is also willing to hold more RED when the rate is very favorable. Pig has experimented but seen that lopsided trades against partners’ obvious preferences get rejected. With only one proposal left for each player and no future to preserve inventory for, each now focuses on immediate, safe gains: dolphin tries to convert low-value RED to BLUE or GREEN one last time, frog looks for any cheap RED acquisition or small improvement involving giving away BLUE or possibly GREEN, and pig looks for small arbitrage where his valuation advantage in BLUE over frog or his symmetric valuation in RED/BLUE can yield a modest sure profit. However, because beliefs are now sharper, low-surplus or mispriced offers are more likely to be declined.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone holds 10 of each chip. No public information about others’ valuations exists yet, so proposals are guided by private calculators and generic expectations. Duck moves first and, being cautious and resource-protective, is unlikely to propose a very aggressive offer; she will test the water with a mild trade that benefits her slightly if accepted. Lion moves second and is more demanding in terms of fairness and self-benefit; he may attempt a somewhat favorable trade for himself, but without yet exploiting the full potential surplus. Tiger moves third and already knows, privately, that BLUE and RED are highly valuable for him; he will likely seek to acquire BLUE cheaply in exchange for GREEN, but has to begin with conservative quantities and accept that his first proposal may be declined while others learn from it.

Going into round 1, the only executed trade so far is tiger’s purchase of 2 BLUE from lion for 2 GREEN in round 0. This reveals publicly that at least lion was willing to part with BLUE at a 1:1 rate for GREEN, and that tiger was willing to give up GREEN for BLUE at that same rate. Duck sees that others are open to trading BLUE and GREEN symmetrically, and may adjust her expectations modestly. Lion now holds slightly fewer BLUE and more GREEN, and might see himself as having made a reasonable deal. Tiger now has more BLUE and fewer GREEN and is confirmed in his strategy that acquiring BLUE for GREEN is feasible. Duck has modestly reshuffled her portfolio via a small BLUE–GREEN swap with lion and a larger BLUE sale to tiger for GREEN; she has seen that selling BLUE for GREEN can be profitable without obvious downside, though she still does not know anyone else’s exact values. Lion has both bought and sold BLUE and GREEN, learning that his counterparts are willing to move these colors at parity, but his attempts to ask for more favorable ratios in GREEN-for-RED or GREEN-for-BLUE trades were rebuffed, reinforcing that aggressive asks get declined. Uncertainty remains around how aggressively each player tries to infer others’ values versus simply proposing locally profitable trades according to their calculator; given the priors, they lean toward straightforward positive-surplus deals rather than intricate signaling or brinkmanship.

rounds.round_explanation: At the very start, inventories are symmetric (10 of each color), but values differ. Ram’s $1.00 blue value versus Bear’s and Goose’s low blue values ($0.30 and $0.20) and high $1.00 red values create obvious surplus opportunities: Ram wants blue, Bear and Goose want red. With no trade history yet, all players are uncertain about others’ valuations, so initial proposals are likely to be simple and somewhat generous, both to test the water and to ensure acceptance. Because all three are fairly cooperative and tolerant of mild inequality, responders will readily accept any offer that gives them even modest positive surplus. This round should begin the flow of blue toward Ram and red toward Bear/Goose, with green used sparingly or as a neutral balancing chip.

By the start of Round 1, Ram has already traded away substantial red for blue, revealing a strong relative preference for blue. Bear and Goose have both acquired extra red and lost blue, hinting that they are willing to pay blue to gain red. This establishes a clear pattern: Ram as a blue-hoarder, Bear and Goose as red-seekers. The easy high-surplus red-for-blue trades are partially exploited but not exhausted; remaining opportunities still exist but are smaller. Proposers can now make more targeted trades, expecting Ram to be the most eager counterparty for blue exchanges and slightly adjusting terms to retain a better margin. Given their cooperative and low-rejection tendencies, responders will still accept modest positive-surplus trades, but proposers may start to shave their offers to capture more surplus while banking on acceptance due to already-established preferences.

Entering the final round, the large initial misalignments are mostly narrowed. Bear and Goose have both improved their red holdings relative to blue, while Ram has accumulated net blue but has already traded back some blue for red when terms were favorable. Everyone now has clearer implicit beliefs about others’ preferences: Ram values blue highly but is not willing to bleed too much blue for red, and Bear/Goose value red more than blue but will not accept strongly negative deals. With only three proposal turns left, players focus on small, safe, positive-surplus trades, potentially using green as a near-neutral chip to fine-tune inventories. Strategic uncertainty remains, but the cooperative, low-threshold personalities make it likely that at least one more mutually beneficial, modest-margin trade is executed, while clearly lopsided proposals get declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features private values on red and blue chips, a common value on green chips, and random matching when multiple players accept a proposal. Each player starts with 10 of each color. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, while red and blue are more valuable to octopus than to the others (octopus values both red and blue at $1.00, lion values red at $0.50 and blue at $0.70, ram values red at $0.20 and blue at $0.70). This creates clear surplus opportunities when octopus trades green for red/blue with lion or ram, and when lion or ram exchange red for blue or vice versa in a way that suits their private valuations. However, players do not know each other’s exact valuations; they only see their own. Each proposal must offer one color and request another, and execution depends on at least one other player accepting. If both non-senders accept, the realized partner is chosen randomly, which creates strategic uncertainty: a responder might be wary of accepting if another responder’s incentives seem even stronger, because bidding too aggressively for an obviously valuable trade could mean getting nothing but revealing preferences. Given the personas, lion and octopus are somewhat prosocial and willing to propose trades that leave others with visible gains, whereas ram is more self-protective and less inclined to give up high-value chips. Over three rounds, we expect experimentation early—testing willingness to trade greens for red/blue—followed by more targeted proposals that exploit observed patterns of acceptance. The main tension is between exploiting win–win red/green and blue/green swaps (especially with octopus as buyer of red/blue) and coordinating around who gets to consummate trades when multiple responders benefit. As the game progresses and remaining rounds dwindle, players become more willing to propose deals that are good for them even if only marginally beneficial for at least one counterpart, because there is no carryover beyond this standalone game.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, everyone starts with symmetric holdings and no information about others’ private values, beyond the public ranges. Lion and octopus are inclined to explore mutually beneficial trades; ram is more guarded. We expect early proposals to be relatively small and framed around the common-value green chip as an anchor, since all know it is worth $0.50 to everyone. Proposals that ask for red or blue in exchange for green are natural first tests: they are easy for responders to evaluate and do not require fine-tuned beliefs about others’ valuations. This round is mainly about discovery: who seems willing to trade which colors, and at what implicit prices.

After round 0, lion has learned that octopus is willing to trade away blue for green and that ram is willing to give up some red for green. Octopus saw lion accept a generous red-for-green offer from ram, suggesting lion is not hoarding green and views red as at least moderately valuable. Ram learned that lion reliably accepts green-for-red trades that leave lion ahead. Now, with updated holdings, the players face an environment where lion is slightly heavier in green and red, octopus is slightly poorer in green and richer in blue, and ram has fewer red and more green. We expect more targeted proposals: lion trying to turn excess green into blue or red, octopus trying again to secure red while not overpaying in green, and ram perhaps attempting a cautious trade that converts less-valued red into either green or blue at a favorable rate.

By the final round, patterns are clearer. Octopus has demonstrated a strong appetite for red, ram has once accepted a favorable blue-for-red offer, and lion has only accepted trades that clearly increase their total value. There is no future beyond this round, so players no longer worry about reputation or future opportunities; they simply try to extract any remaining gains from trade. Lion, still relatively prosocial but value-conscious, is willing to propose deals that give others a visible gain if lion gains enough. Octopus is focused on consolidating high-value red and blue even if that means giving up some green or low-value color. Ram will accept trades only if they offer clear value improvement, given a cautious and self-protective profile. Because it is the last opportunity, marginally positive deals that might have been passed up earlier are now more attractive, but players still reject any trade that is negative in value for them individually.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player, three-round bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Everyone starts with 10 of each color. Green is valued at $0.50 by all players, but red/blue values are asymmetric: buffalo (B) values red at $0.60 and blue at $0.10, cat (C) values both red and blue at $1.00, and duck (D) values red at $1.00 and blue at $0.70. The key efficiency logic is that chips should flow from low-value holders to high-value holders at prices between their valuations, but the players only see their own values. They can, however, infer relative preferences from what others try to buy and sell.

Because acquisitive, self-maximizing behavior is incentivized and random partner selection occurs if multiple players accept, proposers face two main strategic tensions: (1) They want to structure offers that are profitable given their own private values, and (2) they must make the offers attractive enough that at least one other player strictly gains, without being so generous that they regret the trade. All players are risk averse to outright losses and have a surplus calculator, so they will virtually never accept negative-surplus trades for themselves. Given these parameters and the personas, B and D are somewhat tolerant of unequal outcomes and may accept smaller gains, while C is more selective and less trusting.

In equilibrium-like reasoning, C and D will focus on acquiring red from B and trading away green or (for D) some blue that might be less valuable to others. B’s best role is as a supplier of red (and perhaps buyer of blue if the price is favorable), but with uncertainty about others’ valuations B may begin with conservative exploratory offers that slightly favor the counterparty to ensure acceptance. C is likely to pursue aggressive but positive-sum trades, using green (common value) as a standard currency; C is unlikely to give away red or blue cheaply. D, being numerate and tolerant of inequality, will opportunistically accept any small positive gain and structure proposals to test others’ willingness to trade, especially involving blue (which D values more than B but less than C). As the game has only three rounds and each player proposes three times, there is limited scope to fully learn others’ values, so many offers will be slightly mispriced but still positive-sum. Expect some early exploratory trades (especially involving green for red), a couple of clear surplus-creating accepted trades, and a few declined proposals that are either too one-sided or misaligned with counterpart valuations.

In the forecast below, the game features: (1) B offering red for green at a slightly generous rate to attract C or D; (2) C selling green for red or blue at prices that are profitable yet attractive to others; (3) D offering blue or green for red, or occasionally selling higher-valued chips if the rate seems attractive enough from D’s perspective. Acceptances are driven by private surplus positivity and by risk attitudes consistent with the personas, with players typically rejecting break-even or negative trades and accepting modest gains even if unequal.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, players have symmetric endowments and no trade history. Proposals are exploratory and informative: they reveal which colors each player wants to buy or sell and at what implicit exchange rates. Because no one knows others’ private red/blue values, they will tentatively propose trades that are clearly profitable for themselves yet plausibly attractive to others. B, who slightly favors red over green and heavily disfavors blue, is likely to try to acquire green or blue cheaply with red. C, who values red and blue highly, will likely use green to buy red or blue. D, valuing red highly and blue moderately, will likely offer blue or green to get red at modest rates. Responses will be based on immediate surplus gains, with low thresholds for accepting small positive gains, especially for D and B.

By the start of round 1, the history reveals that D was willing to give up green for red at a 1:1 rate and that C is eager to buy red with green, while B did not accept those early offers. B and D now hold more red and less green relative to C. This suggests to others that C values red highly and that D is flexible with green. B infers that offering red for green at a modest premium is acceptable to D; C infers that D will pay green for red and might accept trades involving blue; D infers that C has strong demand for red and may overpay with green. Players will now adjust offers to be slightly more targeted: B will again try to monetize excess red, C will continue to trade green for red or blue but may test slightly less generous rates, and D will attempt to arbitrage by trading green or blue into high-value red, while also being mindful of reducing green too much.

Entering round 2, trade history shows repeated willingness by C and D to trade green for red, and a successful trade where B accepted green for blue at a favorable rate. Everyone has now seen that: (1) C values red and blue strongly and uses green as a trade currency; (2) D readily trades green for red and has moderate valuation of blue; (3) B strongly dislikes holding blue and is happy to sell it for green. With only one round left, players become more opportunistic, focusing on clearly positive-surplus, closing trades rather than probing. B will likely try to unload remaining blue or slightly excess red for green at attractive rates. C may attempt to secure more red or blue, but must avoid depleting green below comfortable levels. D will consider any final opportunity to acquire red at a modest multiple of green or blue, but may reject offers that sharply worsen D’s mix this late. Rejections now mainly occur when a proposal tilts too far in favor of the proposer given what others have inferred about valuations.

Bat values RED and BLUE at $0.80, Fox values BLUE highly at $0.90 and RED weakly at $0.40, and Frog values BLUE most at $1.00 and RED moderately at $0.50. Given that GREEN is exactly $0.50 to everyone, it functions as a neutral baseline that’s rarely attractive to trade unless tied to a shift from a low-value chip into a high-value chip. The core efficiency logic pushes BLUE chips toward Frog (who values them most), then Fox, then Bat, and pushes RED chips toward Bat (highest value) and away from Fox (lowest value). However, each player only knows their own valuations, so they initially infer relative preferences from observed responses rather than exact numbers. Proposals that generate a clear positive surplus for at least one partner but don’t look overly generous are likely. Because responses are simultaneous and random tie-breaking determines the partner when multiple accept, proposers must craft offers that are safe regardless of which responder accepts. Given the prosocial but non-punitive profiles, players are willing to accept even small positive gains and less inclined to reject on fairness grounds. Over three rounds (nine total proposals), they can adjust based on who accepts early trades, gradually revealing their preferences and converging on modestly surplus-improving bilateral exchanges that move BLUE toward Fox and Frog and RED toward Bat, while avoiding obviously negative trades for themselves (which the on-screen surplus calculator highlights).

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, everyone is fully ignorant of others’ specific values and must probe the market. Bat moves first and may cautiously test RED-for-BLUE trades, expecting that at least one other player might like BLUE more or less than she does. Fox, strongly valuing BLUE and weakly valuing RED, is inclined to trade away RED for BLUE if she can, but she doesn’t yet know that others value RED more; she may start with a moderate GREEN or BLUE request that seems fair. Frog, who values BLUE the most, will tend to accept any trade that cheaply increases BLUE or offloads RED for a small concession. Because no history exists yet, the first few proposals are experiments to learn who is willing to move which colors at what prices.

After round 0, everyone has observed that Frog willingly traded away RED for BLUE, revealing that Frog likely values BLUE more than RED. Bat also saw no one eager to part with BLUE for an even GREEN swap, hinting that BLUE is generally not viewed as inferior to GREEN. In this second round, Bat can reasonably guess that offering RED to Fox might be attractive, because Fox refused to give up BLUE cheaply and Frog soaked up RED in exchange for BLUE. Fox now knows at least Frog prizes BLUE and is willing to dump RED, so Fox can try to acquire more BLUE from Frog or Bat using some combination of GREEN and RED. Frog, having already improved his BLUE holdings, may probe whether anyone will cheaply give him more BLUE or accept RED in exchange for GREEN, but now faces more skeptical counterparts who have learned that others also like BLUE.

By the final round, the pattern is clearer: Frog has repeatedly accepted offers that increase BLUE even when it requires giving up RED or GREEN, and Fox has shown willingness to trade away RED for BLUE and to accept neutral GREEN-for-RED trades when they do not reduce her payoff. Bat appears cautious and interested in RED, but not desperate to trade BLUE away. With limited time left, players are inclined to propose only trades that are safely profitable for themselves and plausibly acceptable to at least one other player given past behavior. This leads to conservative, surplus-positive but modest proposals—especially RED-for-GREEN or RED-for-BLUE swaps that move RED from Fox to Bat and BLUE from Bat to Frog or Fox, aligning chip allocations with relative valuations before the game ends.
