parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features two high-value players for blue and red chips (parrot-pink-5400 and turtle-red-9966) and one relatively flat-valuing player (dog-green-8079). Green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so they function as a transparent medium of exchange and a natural benchmark. Parrot and turtle both value blue at $1.00 and red at $0.80 and $0.60 respectively, making red and especially blue more valuable to them than to dog, who values red at $0.50 and blue at $0.30. Dog is therefore naturally positioned as a potential intermediary who can profit by trading away blue and red to the others in exchange for green, or by buying under-valued blue from those who might misestimate its value relative to green. However, all players see exact personal gains or losses for each potential trade through the surplus calculator, so there is very little room for systematic mispricing: any accepted trade must yield a weakly positive gain to the accepting party, and usually strictly positive because there is no fairness enforcement requirement. The main frictions are (1) competition between parrot and turtle over the same valuable chips, (2) stochastic partner selection when both accept, and (3) the short three-round window limiting the scope for elaborate multi-step strategies. Given their personas, dog is flexible and tolerant of unequal splits, likely to accept small but positive gains and to propose mutually beneficial trades. Parrot is prosocial and also tolerant of inequality, so willing to take modest personal gains that clearly help others. Turtle is more self-protective and dislikes uncertainty, so she is likely to accept only clearly beneficial, simple trades and may avoid proposals that depend on competition with parrot. Across the three rounds, we should see early exploratory trades that convert some green into high-value red or blue for parrot and turtle, often using dog as a counterparty, followed by more targeted attempts to correct obvious misallocations (e.g., dog holding too many red or blue relative to his low valuations). Some advantageous trades will still be declined because they help both non-senders, who then face a lottery over who actually gets the gains. That uncertainty, together with risk aversion especially for turtle, can cause good but contested offers to be rejected, particularly late in the game when inventory positions are already improved and incremental gains are smaller.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone holds an equal endowment of 10 chips of each color. No one knows others’ private values, but they know that values differ except for green. Players are likely to begin with simple, symmetric-looking trades that test counterparties’ willingness to exchange green (the known benchmark) for colored chips. Dog, with relatively low blue value and moderate red value, may experiment by offering red or blue for green, while parrot and turtle, who both value red and blue more than green, will want to accumulate those colors. Early in the game, proposals are exploratory, and acceptance decisions are guided primarily by the surplus calculator rather than by elaborate speculation about others’ types. Because both parrot and turtle gain from acquiring colored chips from dog, the main tension is that when dog offers a beneficial trade to both, each must decide whether to join a lottery over who gets it or abstain and hope for a better bilateral opportunity later.

By round 1, two trades have executed: dog has traded away some low-value blue to parrot for green, and then sold red to turtle for green. Dog now holds extra green and somewhat fewer red/blue, parrot has slightly more blue and fewer green, and turtle has gained red at the cost of green. The pattern reveals that both parrot and turtle are willing to pay green to acquire colored chips, which is consistent with them valuing red and blue highly. Dog infers that his role as supplier of colored chips for green is profitable and low-risk. Parrot, having already cheaply improved her blue holdings, now looks for red, while turtle may look to acquire more blue or maintain her red advantage without giving up too much green. Everyone has learned that fair-looking green-for-color trades get accepted, so proposals remain relatively balanced, though they can now be more sharply targeted at perceived shortages (parrot’s red, turtle’s blue). Uncertainty centers on whether dog can extract slightly better terms now that he sees demand for colored chips, and whether parrot or turtle would risk entering lotteries over offers that benefit them both.

Entering the final round, dog has repeatedly exchanged colored chips for green, steadily converting lower-value inventory into common-value assets at modest positive margins. Parrot has improved both blue and red holdings at the cost of green but still retains enough green to contemplate one more beneficial trade. Turtle has picked up additional red and blue while spending green, consistent with her preference for high-value but safe-seeming deals. Everyone now has clearer beliefs that dog’s valuations for red and blue are lower than the others’, since he keeps selling them for green, while parrot and turtle keep buying. This convergence of beliefs makes further win-win trades possible but narrower: dog is running low on the most in-demand chips, and parrot and turtle are more cautious about surrendering their remaining green too cheaply. In the last three turns, players prioritize simple, clearly positive trades over speculative or lottery-contingent ones, as there is no future game in which to recoup a small loss. Some potentially profitable but more aggressive offers are declined because the incremental gain is not worth the risk or perceived unfairness in the final state.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone holds 10 of each chip color and has no observation of others' preferences. Dog, a very prosocial and uncertainty-averse player, opens with a cautious, slightly self-favoring offer that they believe could still be attractive. Mouse follows with a similarly modest-value proposal. Owl, who values BLUE the most and is more strategic, uses their first proposal to begin testing whether others are willing to part with BLUE in exchange for RED at a ratio that still benefits Owl. By the start of round 1, Dog and Mouse have each seen one of their low-margin offers declined, suggesting that 1:1 trades in low-surplus directions are not enticing. Mouse infers that someone likely values BLUE more than RED, while Owl learns that at least one partner is willing to part with BLUE for RED even at neutral apparent value. Mouse, having just traded with Owl, tentatively tries to reverse some of the flow in a slightly self-favoring way. Owl, recognizing it is the last opportunity, offers an attractive BLUE-for-GREEN exchange that leverages Owl’s strong BLUE demand and common GREEN value to entice acceptance.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game is effectively about converting lower-value chips into higher-value ones, under incomplete information about others’ private values and with random matching on each accepted proposal. All players share the same value for GREEN chips ($0.50), but differ slightly on RED and BLUE: alligator and buffalo value RED=$1.00 and BLUE=$0.70, while rabbit values RED=$0.90 and BLUE=$0.60. So, for every player, RED > BLUE > GREEN in value, and alligator/buffalo care slightly more about color differences than rabbit.

Because each chip type’s public range is broad ($0.10–$1.00 for RED/BLUE) and players know only their own exact values, each player is uncertain about how much others value each color. They can infer that GREEN is exactly $0.50 for everyone but must guess whether others see RED or BLUE as closer to the high or low end of the public interval. This uncertainty supports mutually profitable trades: one player may treat BLUE as relatively cheap while another treats RED as only modestly more expensive.

The structure strongly encourages incremental, low-risk proposals: each player has a calculator that shows their own surplus from any contemplated trade. Rational, value-maximizing players will almost never propose negative-surplus trades for themselves or accept negative-surplus trades from others. Surplus-neutral trades (0 gain) are possible but psychologically unattractive unless a player is trying to explore others’ preferences. Thus, accepted trades should nearly always be strictly positive surplus for both sides ex post.

Given the valuations, classic win–win trades look like offering BLUE or GREEN to acquire RED, or sometimes swapping BLUE for GREEN if someone (incorrectly) infers a different GREEN value for others. Here all three rank the colors identically, so true, strict Pareto improvements require differential intensities or small mistakes driven by uncertainty. However, because the public range allows for the belief that others might value specific colors lower or higher than oneself, players may opportunistically propose trades that look fair or symmetric in chip counts but are slightly in their own favor given their private values.

Personas shape likely behavior: alligator is self-protective but comfortable with inequality, likely to seek clearly favorable trades and to accept any strictly beneficial deal, even if it seems lopsided for others. Buffalo is prosocial, somewhat fairness-oriented, and risk-averse, inclined toward balanced or gently asymmetric trades that visibly help both sides, and reluctant to accept risky or marginal offers. Rabbit is also prosocial and tolerant of unequal splits but cognitively sharp, likely to calculate carefully and accept even small positive gains, prioritizing efficiency over fairness concerns.

Because multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers cannot target a specific partner. That encourages framing proposals so that they are acceptable to at least one counterpart but not too attractive to both if the proposer views some counterpart as more exploitable or less informed. In this symmetric, one-shot 3-round structure without explicit end-of-game salvage value changes, discounting is minimal; all three rounds can support trades. However, players may expect that others become more willing to accept smaller gains in later rounds, so early proposals may be slightly more ambitious, while later proposals trend toward more obviously mutually beneficial trades to ensure that some surplus is realized before the game ends.

Overall, we expect: (1) modest, one-color-for-one-color proposals centered on RED↔BLUE or RED↔GREEN exchanges; (2) most accepted trades to move chips toward players who already value them more, generating small but positive surplus; (3) buffalo and rabbit to be somewhat more willing than alligator to accept small positive gains that also benefit others, but all players to avoid clear personal losses; and (4) at least one accepted trade per round, with some proposals being declined as too stingy or miscalibrated under value uncertainty.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have symmetric inventories (10 of each color) but know only their own exact values. Strategic focus is on exploratory but safe trades that reveal something about others’ preferences without risking losses. Alligator, moving first, is likely to test whether anyone is willing to part with BLUE or GREEN for RED at a rate that’s good for him. Buffalo and rabbit, both prosocial but careful, will respond based on whether offers give them clear positive value. Given full surplus calculators, most early proposals that are too skewed will be declined, but slightly favorable or fair-looking trades can be accepted as players tentatively reallocate chips toward their preferred colors.

By the start of round 1, rabbit has gained some BLUE and RED at the expense of GREEN, while alligator and buffalo have each traded away some BLUE in exchange for preferred colors. The public history suggests that rabbit is willing to accept modest but positive gains involving giving up GREEN, while buffalo declined some earlier offers that weren’t clearly beneficial. Players now update beliefs: rabbit may be seen as flexible but careful; buffalo as prosocial but not exploitable; alligator as value-focused. In this middle round, proposers are likely to refine offers toward more obviously win–win ratios, using smaller quantities to avoid situations where multiple players would want to accept the same trade, and to avoid giving away too much RED without clear compensation.

Entering the final round, the remaining chips are modestly reallocated: rabbit is somewhat RED-heavy but has spent GREEN and BLUE; buffalo has a more balanced portfolio with slight shifts toward GREEN and away from BLUE; alligator has acquired some additional RED by giving up BLUE and GREEN in earlier rounds. With no future beyond this game, time pressure pushes players toward any strictly positive-sum trades they can find. Risk-averse buffalo is now more willing to accept even small gains to avoid leaving surplus unrealized. Alligator, tolerant of unequal outcomes, is still focused on extracting value but may accept thin-margin trades if there are no better options. Rabbit, prosocial and calculating, is comfortable with small but clearly positive gains. The last few proposals thus tend to be simple, low-quantity RED↔BLUE or RED↔GREEN swaps that smooth out remaining inefficiencies, though some attempts may still be declined if the surplus on one side is negligible or if a player perceives the offer as exploiting their earlier revealed willingness to trade.

All players start with 10 of each color, so there are no initial inventory imbalances. The key driver of surplus is that goose and buffalo both value red chips more than blue, and more than bear does, while buffalo values blue quite low. This creates potential gains from trade if red can be shifted toward goose and buffalo, and blue shifted away from buffalo, but any trade must be privately beneficial to both parties involved, and the sender never sees others’ values. Uncertainty and strategic tension come from the fact that each player only knows their own values and infers others’ preferences from observable behavior across turns. Goose (highly logical, tolerant of inequality) is motivated to accumulate red cheaply but will not sacrifice value; bear (more reciprocal, moderate fairness concern) is roughly indifferent between red and blue and may try mild exploitation if she senses others overvalue red; buffalo (highly numerical, cooperative but not naive) wants red more than blue but will quickly reject disadvantageous terms. Because green has identical value to all, proposals involving green tend to be used as a neutral currency or as cheap screening offers, but any such trade that moves green for red or blue must still give at least weakly positive private surplus to the accepting party. Over three short rounds, there is limited time to learn, so players will lean on simple, transparent offers that they expect could plausibly be accepted without giving away too much, leading to a few modest, value-creating trades and several rejected attempts as they probe each other’s willingness to exchange red and blue.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has yet revealed preferences, so each proposer is effectively probing. Goose moves first and is likely to test appetite for green versus a more valuable color, while still ensuring her own non-negative surplus. Bear then gets a chance to feel out how others value red relative to green. Buffalo, observing two turns of behavior, can update beliefs slightly and may cautiously test a red-for-blue swap that is good for her but not too extreme, anticipating that others might also like red.

By the start of round 1, goose and buffalo have executed a mutually beneficial trade, both confirming that they each value red more than blue and implicitly signaling that blue is relatively cheap to both of them compared to red. Bear has seen these terms and learns that at least goose was willing to give up one red for two blue, which suggests strong red preference. Players will now refine offers: goose can try to keep accumulating red from buffalo or bear using her surplus of blue; bear may probe again for mildly favorable red-for-blue swaps; buffalo, now slightly lower on blue and higher on red, becomes more selective, trying to avoid giving away too much red while still exploiting her low valuation of blue when it’s profitable.

Entering the final round, goose has demonstrated a clear willingness to exchange blue for red at roughly two blue per red when it yields her positive surplus, and both counterparties have occasionally rejected mildly unfavorable terms. Everyone now has some information: others value red at least weakly more than blue, but not enough to accept strictly negative own-value trades. With only three turns left, proposers face a last chance to squeeze out beneficial exchanges. Goose, already holding extra red relative to start, might attempt one more red-for-blue acquisition that is clearly attractive to at least one partner. Bear, having only one successful earlier trade and seeing others’ reluctance to take small losses, may either offer a nearly fair or slightly generous deal. Buffalo, with strong numerical skills and now decent information about goose’s red hunger, might float a marginally favorable trade but will quickly abandon aggressive attempts that seem unlikely to be accepted in this last opportunity.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player, three-round chip-bargaining game creates a small exchange economy with common-value green chips ($0.50 for everyone) and privately valued red/blue chips. All players start with 10 of each color. Because green is common-value and symmetric, it mainly serves as a safe medium of exchange. The main surplus opportunities come from trading red and blue across players who value them differently.

Private values:
- Peacock: RED $0.80, BLUE $0.50, GREEN $0.50.
- Cat: RED $0.50, BLUE $0.30, GREEN $0.50.
- Otter: RED $0.70, BLUE $0.20, GREEN $0.50.

From an omniscient perspective, red is most valuable to Peacock, then Otter, then Cat; blue is most valuable to Peacock, then Cat, then Otter. Green is neutral and identical. The best gains come from Peacock buying red from Cat and Otter (who value red less), and from Cat buying blue from Otter (who values blue least) and possibly from Peacock if Peacock gets a very favorable rate. Otter, with low blue and mid red, wants to trade away blue for green or red when the rate is clearly positive.

However, each player only sees their own valuations, so they infer others’ values through offers and responses. Everyone has a surplus calculator that displays their own net gain/loss for any proposal, so they will not accept strictly negative trades. Uncertainty is about how others value each color and about whom a proposal will actually match with if both potential recipients accept.

Personas imply all three are moderately prosocial, dislike uncertainty, and are not strongly punitive. Peacock is tolerant of inequality and wants clear structure; Cat is highly trusting and cooperative but not necessarily generous in ultimatum offers; Otter is cooperative in reciprocation but very reluctant to give away value unilaterally. This suggests: (1) proposals will generally be conservative, aiming for clear positive surplus to others to increase acceptance; (2) players will adjust quickly when they see what others accept; and (3) nobody will sacrifice own value merely to enforce fairness norms.

Over three rounds, with fixed proposer order, we should see early exploratory offers using green as a safe medium, then more targeted trades: Cat and Otter shedding blue for green or red, Peacock trying to acquire red while not giving away too much blue or green. Because trades execute immediately and inventory constraints bind, earlier trades shape later bargaining by changing who still has surplus to trade. Random selection among multiple accepters slightly discourages ultra-targeted offers; senders will design deals good enough that either partner is acceptable, or they will skew rates to favor the partner who is more likely to see good surplus. The game will likely see a handful of mutually profitable small trades rather than large, risky swaps.

rounds.round_explanation:
Round 0 is fully symmetric in endowments (10 of each color) and information but asymmetric in preferences. No one yet knows how the others value each chip, only that ranges are between $0.10 and $1.00 for red/blue and $0.50 fixed for green. As first movers, players will likely try small, low-risk trades that are visibly profitable to potential partners, often using green as a medium to avoid accidentally offering others a loss. Because multiple accepters are randomized, senders in this first round design offers that would be acceptable regardless of which rival accepts, while still improving their own portfolio. We should see gentle price discovery: proposals that test whether others are eager to get rid of blue or red and how they respond to particular exchange rates.

With these priors, we forecast Peacock first trying to cheaply acquire red or green by offering blue at a slightly favorable rate to others; Cat then probing trades that improve their red or green holdings while unloading some blue; and Otter using their turn to convert low-value blue into either green or red at clearly positive surplus for themselves.

At the start of Round 1, inventories reflect Round 0 trades. Publicly observed outcomes tell everyone that: (a) neither Cat nor Otter was willing to trade 2 green for 2 blue at parity, hinting they value blue at not much more than green or even less; (b) Cat and Otter were both willing to join a 3 BLUE for 2 RED trade at least for one side, so both appear to value red enough relative to blue; and (c) Peacock accepted 2 BLUE for 1 GREEN, signaling green is not higher-valued than blue for Peacock and that Peacock is willing to gather blue if the price is right. These signals shift expectations: blue is generally low-value, red is relatively higher-value, and green is a safe middle.

Given this, Cat (now first mover) is motivated to keep improving red holdings or lock in green while reducing blue. Peacock, going second, has learned that Otter devalues blue heavily and may see Otter as a good target to swap blue into green or red. Otter, moving last, knows both others will not give away red cheaply but may still unload blue. Players will propose smaller, more targeted trades with clearer net gains for both sides, aware that time (rounds) is limited.

By Round 2, a pattern is clear: blue has repeatedly been sold off by Cat and Otter and willingly absorbed by Peacock at sufficiently favorable rates, while red has been in demand by all and traded only when compensated by multiple blue. Green has served as a stabilizing token, moving mostly from Peacock to others when they unload blue, and from others back toward those shedding blue at the end of Round 1. Everyone now has informative observations about relative valuations and remaining inventories, and this is the final chance to improve portfolios.

Cat, now with relatively more red and green than at the start and reduced blue, may be reluctant to give up much more red but can still adjust away some residual blue. Peacock, with strong red preferences and a large blue stock, wants to swap blue for red or green but must still leave visible surplus for others. Otter, having repeatedly converted blue into green, may now focus on marginal improvements, possibly trading one color only if the gain is clear. With limited remaining time, proposals become more aggressive on price but smaller in size, as each player fine-tunes holdings without jeopardizing already improved positions.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game revolves around exchanging privately valued red and blue chips plus common-value green chips. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, but red and blue vary sharply: badger values both red and blue highly ($1.00 each), squid strongly prefers blue over red ($1.00 vs $0.10), and camel moderately prefers blue over red ($0.80 vs $0.40). Gains from trade come mainly from moving chips from low-value to high-value holders while compensating them with something they value more. Because recipients know only their own values and not others’ valuations, they will accept any strictly profitable net trades on their screen, even if the overall surplus might be small or even negative for the group. The main bilateral efficiency opportunities are: (1) squid or camel selling red (low for them) to badger (high for him) in exchange for blue or green; (2) camel selling red to squid for blue; and (3) possibly squid giving away green to obtain blue, though green is already fairly valuable. However, each proposal targets both potential recipients simultaneously, and if both accept, the partner is chosen at random, so senders face uncertainty about which counterparty they will trade with. Rational senders will therefore tend to propose trades that are profitable against either possible acceptor or that are structured so only the intended counterparty finds it clearly attractive. Persona-wise, squid is highly reflective, tolerant of unequal splits, and cooperative, so likely to construct near-surplus-maximizing trades that give visible value to others to ensure acceptance. Badger is somewhat strategic but less numerate, cares about being treated reasonably, and will seek simple, clearly profitable offers, particularly those increasing his already valuable red inventory. Camel is trusting, tolerant of inequality, and numerate; he will accept many small profitable trades and is willing to move low-value red chips if the numbers look good. Over the three rounds, we should see early exploratory offers, then convergence toward simple red-for-blue/green trades that transfer red chips to badger and liquidate some blue from squid and camel, as long as each step remains myopically profitable for all involved.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, everyone starts symmetric in holdings (10 of each color) but not in values. No one knows others’ valuations, so initial proposals are likely to be exploratory and fairly generous to potential partners to ensure acceptances. Squid, being highly analytical and tolerant of inequality, is likely to test an obviously win–win configuration that offloads his very low-value red in exchange for common-value green. Badger will then probably try to lock in high-value red by paying with green, aiming at either low-red-valuers. Camel, seeing at least one successful trade, may then experiment with exchanging his relatively low-value red for blue from someone who likely values blue a lot but might still find the trade acceptable because they are paid with green or because it is framed as a mild gain on their calculator.

By the start of the second round, camel has meaningfully shifted his portfolio toward red (from badger) and away from green, and squid has slightly increased his green and reduced his red while gaining some red back from the camel trade. The public history shows that camel has been a very willing counterparty, especially on green-for-red and blue-for-red trades, while squid has also accepted a mutually beneficial blue-for-red trade. Badger has successfully acquired extra red from camel using green and now recognizes camel as a reliable, low-resistance trading partner. This history encourages squid to attempt a more ambitious trade that converts his remaining low-value red into high-value blue, possibly overpaying slightly in green to make the offer appealing to both listeners. Badger, now holding extra red but lower green, will continue to push green-for-red trades, likely again targeting camel. Camel, understanding that he’s been giving away substantial value in red, may attempt to rebalance by trading red away in exchange for blue and simultaneously recovering some green, targeting either squid or badger with terms that still show a modest profit to the recipient.

Entering the final round, patterns are clear: red has been flowing toward badger, blue has been moving primarily toward squid and camel, and camel has consistently accepted slightly favorable trades even when they push his portfolio away from green. All three players now have better informal beliefs about each other’s willingness to trade particular colors, even without knowing exact values. Squid, still with very cheap red and very valuable blue, will attempt a last conversion of red into blue or green if any low-resistance partner has surplus. Badger, having accumulated a large red pile and spent a good amount of green, may now be more selective, preferring only clearly profitable red acquisitions or possibly swapping some excess red for blue if the terms are particularly attractive. Camel, having been the most flexible trader, is likely to continue accepting any final-round offers that show clear on-screen gains, especially ones that restore some green or trade away his low-value red, but he will be mindful that this is the last opportunity to improve his final payoff. This encourages all senders to offer straightforward, obviously profitable deals to secure immediate acceptance rather than experiment.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player, three-round bargaining game is driven by asymmetric private values for red and blue chips, with green chips having a common value of $0.50 to everyone. All players start with 10 of each color. Ram values red and blue highly (RED=$0.90, BLUE=$0.70), Butterfly values them moderately (RED=$0.70, BLUE=$0.50), and Dog values them very little (RED=$0.20, BLUE=$0.10). Because green is worth the same to everyone, surplus comes from Dog trading away under-valued red and blue chips to Ram and Butterfly, who value them more, in exchange for green chips. This structure creates strong directional incentives:

- Ram wants to acquire red and blue and is willing to pay with green, but must be careful not to give away red (their highest-valued chip) unless they get even more value in return.
- Butterfly also benefits from acquiring red (and to a lesser extent blue) from Dog, and will typically be willing to part with some green in exchange, but will be wary of selling red or blue to Ram at a net loss.
- Dog benefits by offloading red and blue, which are relatively low-value, in exchange for green or potentially higher-valued chips from others, and is the natural liquidity provider in red/blue.

Strategically, each player only sees their own values, but they know that others may value colors differently. The UI calculator makes sure no one willingly accepts a trade that they see as a loss, so accepted trades must be weakly positive for both realized parties. However, offers must be attractive to at least one counterparty while still profitable for the proposer, and players face uncertainty about how much others value each color and how aggressively they will trade.

The simultaneous response rule and random selection among multiple accepters creates additional uncertainty: a proposer cannot target a specific partner when both are willing to accept, so trades that are good with one potential counterparty but bad with the other may be risky. Given all three players are relatively tolerant of unequal outcomes and oriented toward cooperation in prior tasks, they are likely to focus on straightforward, obviously positive-sum trades rather than fine-grained strategic signaling.

Across three rounds, with each player proposing once per round, we should see Ram and Butterfly repeatedly attempting to buy red (and sometimes blue) from Dog using green. Dog may occasionally experiment with trying to buy green using their red or blue, but will typically demand too much to get acceptance from Ram or Butterfly. Because everyone starts with a large endowment and there is no penalty for untraded chips, there is no desperation to trade late in the game, but there is an incentive to close clearly profitable deals early so that subsequent proposals can build on improved portfolios. Overall, the most likely pattern is a handful of accepted trades that move red and blue from Dog to Ram and Butterfly in exchange for green, with several declined proposals when someone overreaches or misjudges others' valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each color and have no direct evidence of others' valuations. They know only that values may differ across players. Ram, who values both red and blue highly, will likely start cautiously by offering green for red or blue in a way that is clearly profitable for themselves but plausibly attractive to at least one counterpart. Dog, with low valuations for red and blue, is a likely seller of those colors, but does not yet know that others value them much more, so may initially make fairly balanced or slightly cautious proposals. Butterfly, with moderate valuations, is likely to aim for deals that swap some green for red, especially from Dog, once they see early responses and get some signal about what might be acceptable.

By the start of round 1, players have some evidence: Dog has accepted trades in which they sold red and blue for green, revealing a willingness to part with red/blue at modest prices. Ram and Butterfly can infer that Dog likely values green relatively more or red/blue relatively less. Ram, having already improved their red holdings, may either continue to buy red/blue from Dog or experiment with a slightly more aggressive price to test Dog's threshold. Dog, now holding fewer red and blue and more green, may probe whether others will pay more green for remaining red or blue, or attempt a small speculative trade the other way. Butterfly, seeing previous acceptances, is likely to continue trying to buy red from Dog at a favorable ratio, possibly adjusting quantities to stay clearly in the black while remaining attractive to Dog.

By round 2, the pattern is clear: Dog repeatedly accepts trades where they give red or blue in exchange for green, indicating that Dog places relatively less value on red/blue than at least one counterpart. Ram and Butterfly each have improved portfolios of red and blue and have successfully executed mutually beneficial trades. With only one round left, players face little risk of future retaliation or needing to maintain a reputation, so they may slightly increase their demanded surplus. However, because all three players are generally tolerant of inequality and have benefited from previous trades, they are also likely to repeat successful patterns rather than dramatically change strategy. Ram and Butterfly may each take one more shot at buying red from Dog with green at a ratio that both find acceptable, while Dog likely continues to offload remaining low-value red and blue as long as the calculator shows a gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately known red/blue values and a common green value. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, red is worth $0.20 to lion, $0.50 to iguana, and $0.30 to alligator, and blue is worth $0.20 to lion, $0.70 to iguana, and $0.70 to alligator. All start with 10 of each color. Total chip counts never change; players redistribute colors via trades that must benefit both sides (each player sees their own surplus calculator).

The core efficiency motive is to move red chips toward iguana (highest red value) and away from lion (lowest), and to move blue chips toward iguana and alligator (highest blue value) and away from lion. Green has no trade motive because it is identical in value for all three; it can still be used as a convenient payment token but does not create or destroy surplus by moving between players. From an outside perspective, a surplus-maximizing pattern would concentrate red and blue with iguana and alligator and concentrate relatively less valuable chips with lion. But players only know their own values, so they must infer others’ valuations from which colors they try to buy and which offers they accept.

Lion is relatively trusting and tolerant of unequal outcomes from prior tasks, suggesting willingness to make modest positive-sum offers even if their advantage is small, and not to push hard for perfect fairness. Iguana is cooperative and accepting of very unequal splits, and her high cognitive reflection suggests she will be numerically careful: she is willing to accept small positive gains and exploit clear arbitrage if she sees it. Alligator is more cautious (uncertainty averse) but also cooperative; she is unlikely to accept negative-surplus trades and will want clear numeric justification, but will gladly accept even modest positive gains.

Given the surplus calculator, any accepted trade must give both sides weakly positive private surplus; negative-surplus trades will almost never be accepted. Thus, we expect most proposed trades to be roughly balanced privately and occasionally to be slightly more beneficial to the proposer. Early in the game, players know only their own valuations and the public range for others; they may test the waters with small, exploratory trades that leave both parties slightly up in value. As the game progresses and players observe which colors others are eager to buy or sell, they can update beliefs: for example, repeated attempts by iguana to acquire blue signal that she values blue relatively highly.

Strategically, each proposer wants to exploit their own comparative advantage. Lion is the lowest valuer of both red and blue; she should try to trade away red and blue in exchange for green or the other color she thinks is less useful to others, but since green is equal-value for everyone, lion’s best surplus comes from sending away the colors she personally values least in exchange for colors she personally values more, or by modest arbitrage if she infers others’ different preferences. Iguana and alligator gain by acquiring more red and especially blue from lion in exchange for green or some red/blue mix that leaves them still better off. However, since they don’t know that others value colors differently, they will make conservative proposals: offers that give all sides a visible surplus improvement without requiring strong beliefs about others’ preferences.

Uncertainty mainly concerns others’ private red/blue valuations. Because the game has only three rounds and no direct counteroffers, there is limited time to learn. Players will tend to propose trades that are clearly positive to themselves and plausibly positive for a typical opponent in the public range (e.g., asking for a small amount of a chip while giving up slightly more of another chip so that, given the wide $0.10–$1 range, the other side is unlikely to be hurt). Simultaneous responses and random selection among multiple accepters create mild competition: a proposer might design attractive trades that both other players would accept, but then must accept partner uncertainty. Given the personas, we expect modestly cooperative, low-conflict dynamics: most accepted trades will be small, mutually profitable adjustments rather than aggressive exploitation.

Across the three rounds (nine proposal turns), we expect a few modest accepted trades, mostly moving some red and blue from lion toward iguana and alligator, often with green as a balancing token, and several cautious proposals that get declined when at least one responder sees little or no gain on their surplus calculator. Later in the game, as players glean more information from behavior (e.g., iguana repeatedly requesting red or blue), they may make slightly more targeted proposals that capture more of the surplus while still remaining acceptable to a partner.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each color and only know their own valuations and the public ranges. Lion, who values both red and blue only $0.20, is likely to test trading away some combination of these in exchange for green, which is relatively valuable to her. Iguana and alligator each strongly like blue and moderately value red, but do not yet know lion’s low valuations. They will respond based on their own surplus calculators: they will accept trades that clearly give them more value in their preferred colors, especially blue, without giving up too much green. As proposers, iguana and alligator will likely issue small, exploratory offers that use green to buy red or blue and that look plausibly beneficial to a randomly chosen partner, aiming to uncover who values what while locking in small gains.

Going into round 1, holdings have shifted slightly: lion has traded some red for green and then some green for blue, signaling a mild desire for blue and green over red. Iguana has gained blue from lion and given up green, revealing that she values blue more than green. Alligator has given lion green for red earlier, suggesting she at least does not mind accumulating red if the rate is fair. The players now have weak evidence that blue is valuable to iguana, red is not especially prized by lion, and green is a flexible balancing chip. Proposers this round can refine their offers: lion can try to offload more red (and possibly excess blue) for green or small mixes of other colors; iguana may attempt to buy blue or red from either partner using green; and alligator might propose trades that consolidate her portfolio toward blue and red if affordable. Responses will reflect more targeted expectations: for example, iguana will readily accept gaining blue at favorable rates and decline trades that cost her too much blue or red for green.

By round 2, several trades have revealed more structure: iguana has consistently accepted opportunities to gain blue and, when the rate is reasonable, red; lion has been willing to exchange red and blue for green or other colors when her surplus calculator shows a nontrivial gain; alligator has accepted trades that give her more green and red early on but has been more selective when rates do not favor her. The remaining time horizon is short—only three proposals are left—so players are less concerned about future reputation and more focused on immediate surplus. Proposers will likely attempt a final set of small, clearly positive-sum trades that smooth portfolios toward each player’s perceived strengths: iguana and alligator holding more blue and decent amounts of red, lion holding more green and whichever color is least in demand by others. Because all three have shown willingness to accept modest gains, some final trades are likely to go through, but extremely imbalanced proposals are still expected to be declined as they will show negative surplus on the responder’s calculator.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips (always $0.50 each) and privately valued red/blue chips. All players know the public value ranges but only see their own exact values via the surplus calculator. The core strategic tension is that everyone strongly values red relative to blue except duck, who values red and blue equally low. Specifically: whale values RED the highest ($0.70) and BLUE moderately ($0.30); dolphin values RED and GREEN equally ($0.50) and BLUE low ($0.20); duck values GREEN at $0.50 but RED and BLUE only $0.20. This creates clear gains from trade: whale wants to accumulate red, dolphin wants to move out of blue into red/green, and duck is a natural provider of red to whale and blue to dolphin in exchange for green. However, players do not know each other’s exact valuations, only that the ranges are between $0.10 and $1.00 for red/blue. They infer others’ values gradually from proposals and responses. Because each round gives only one proposal per player and acceptance decisions are simultaneous with random tie-breaking if multiple players accept, proposers must design deals that are attractive enough to at least one counterparty but not so generous that they regret the terms. Given the personas—relatively cooperative, low rejection tendencies, and short time horizons—players are inclined to accept modest but clearly positive deals rather than hold out for perfect information. Over three rounds, a typical path has early small, exploratory trades that reveal who is willing to move which colors, followed by more targeted red-for-green or blue-for-green trades that push chips toward their higher-value holders, with occasional declined offers when the surplus is too skewed or ambiguous for the responders.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip color and have no information about others’ precise red/blue values. Green is known to be common-value at $0.50, so it is a safe medium of exchange. Dolphin moves first and is likely to probe demand for green or red, using a small, low-risk trade to see who is willing to pay green or red for blue. Whale then reacts with her first attempt to acquire red, probably using green as payment since she values green equally to its common value and red even more. Duck, cooperative and tolerant of unequal outcomes, will likely accept mildly favorable or even roughly neutral trades if they are easy to understand, and will also make modest offers rather than extremely aggressive ones. This round primarily serves to discover who is willing to trade what, with small stakes and at least one accepted trade that starts to move chips toward higher-valued holders.

Entering Round 1, holdings reflect two accepted trades from Round 0: dolphin has traded away some blue for green; whale has traded away some green for red; duck has parted with green and red while accumulating blue. This suggests a pattern: dolphin wants less blue and more green, whale clearly wants more red, and duck is willing to give up green and red when compensated with blue. Players still do not know exact valuations but infer that whale values red highly and that duck is flexible. Dolphin now has fewer blue chips left to shed and may attempt a more targeted red-for-blue or red-for-green trade. Whale, having succeeded with duck once, may try a slightly larger or repeated red-for-green trade, while duck, after seeing both others’ willingness to pay for green and red, may experiment with asking for red from whale or green from dolphin at somewhat improved terms. The uncertainty about each other’s reservation values remains, but Round 1 tends to feature somewhat larger trades and a clearer division of roles: whale as red aggregator, dolphin as blue shedder/green seeker, and duck as flexible middle trader.

By Round 2, players have seen several accepted trades: dolphin repeatedly trading away blue, duck often on the receiving side of blue-for-green or blue-for-red deals, and whale focusing on acquiring red and occasionally taking blue for green when the terms look safe. The trade history reveals relative willingness to pay: whale’s strong demand for red, dolphin’s eagerness to offload blue, and duck’s tolerance for stocking blue while giving up some red/green. With only one final proposal each, players now balance the marginal value of further rebalancing against the risk of proposing a trade that no one accepts. They also recognize that others have short horizons and low rejection thresholds, so modest positive deals are likely to be accepted if they are clearly beneficial on the calculator. Dolphin may make a last attempt to convert any remaining blue into red or green. Whale may try one more red-acquisition offer, but if previous blue-for-red attempts failed, she may switch back to green-for-red with duck. Duck may offer a conservative, slightly favorable trade using her accumulated blue to regain green or red. The final pattern typically locks in modest efficiency improvements without fully exhausting all gains from trade, due to limited rounds and lingering private information.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric inventories (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but asymmetric private values for RED and BLUE, while GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Zebra values RED highly ($0.80) and BLUE moderately ($0.30), Gorilla values BLUE more than RED ($0.40 vs. $0.20), and Turtle values BLUE very highly ($0.70) and RED very little ($0.10). This creates strong cross-player gains from trade in RED↔BLUE swaps: Zebra wants to dump BLUE for RED, Gorilla wants to turn some RED into BLUE, and Turtle especially wants BLUE and is willing to give up low-value RED or even some GREEN in favorable terms. GREEN is a safe medium-valued chip for all and acts as a neutral currency that can bridge disagreements about RED vs. BLUE. However, players only know their own valuations and the public range for each color, not the others’ exact numbers. This incomplete information plus random partner selection (when multiple responders accept) leads to uncertainty about how generous to be and when to risk multi-acceptance. Persona-wise, Zebra is strongly prosocial and uncertainty-averse, inclined to propose fair-looking, modest surplus trades that are very likely to be accepted and to avoid complex or highly exploitative offers. Gorilla is tolerant of unequal splits, flexible, and patient; they will frequently accept any non-negative deal and may propose slightly self-favoring but not extreme terms. Turtle is relatively analytical, moderately prosocial, and somewhat fairness-sensitive, especially about not ending up with very poor deals. Over three short rounds, no one can fully learn the others’ actual values, so proposals will likely be simple, anchored in the public ranges, and often framed as roughly even in face value to appear reasonable. The dominant economic pattern is RED flowing toward Zebra, BLUE flowing toward Gorilla and especially Turtle, and GREEN sometimes used as a balancing chip; many favorable trades will be accepted, but some borderline or lopsided offers will be rejected, especially early on before players see who is willing to move which colors.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, everyone holds 10 of each color and has no history about the others’ preferences. Zebra, being prosocial and uncertainty-averse, is likely to start with a simple, seemingly fair trade using GREEN as a neutral bridge, trying not to offend or confuse anyone. Gorilla will watch this first move to infer how responsive others are and then propose a modestly self-beneficial but acceptable deal, likely involving swapping some of their low-value RED for either BLUE or GREEN. Turtle will be cautious but willing to trade if the calculator shows a clear gain, and may test the waters with a small RED-for-BLUE proposal that looks fair in dollar terms. Because everyone is just starting to infer others’ patterns, round-0 offers are small, symmetric-looking, and rarely extreme; rejections mainly occur when a responder’s calculator shows a clear loss or trivial gain relative to the cognitive effort or perceived fairness.

By the beginning of round 1, Zebra has seen that a seemingly generous RED-for-GREEN attempt drew no takers, which she may interpret as others not being eager to part with GREEN or being conservative early. Gorilla successfully traded RED for BLUE with Turtle, learning that Turtle is willing to accept RED if the BLUE terms are good enough, and that Zebra is more hesitant. Turtle has revealed a strong appetite for BLUE (accepting Gorilla’s 3 RED for 2 BLUE) and failed to get additional BLUE for GREEN from the others, suggesting that the others value GREEN more or at least won’t give up BLUE cheaply. Given these signals, Zebra will now be more careful and may try a more balanced-looking RED-for-BLUE trade that offers clear dollar gains to at least one counterpart. Gorilla, having benefited from the earlier swap, will continue to offload RED for either BLUE or GREEN, possibly pushing slightly more self-favoring terms now that they know Turtle is responsive. Turtle, noticing that BLUE is in demand and that others are wary of giving it away, might try to acquire a bit more BLUE from Zebra (who values RED highly) with a trade that looks close to even but grants Turtle a small surplus.

Going into the final round, some redistributions have occurred: Zebra has traded away some RED for GREEN and BLUE, Gorilla has converted multiple cheap RED chips into higher-value BLUE and some GREEN, and Turtle has increased BLUE holdings at the cost of RED. Everyone now has a clearer qualitative sense that Zebra likes RED, Gorilla and especially Turtle like BLUE, and GREEN is valued fairly steadily by all. With only one proposal left per player, there is an incentive to clear any remaining obviously beneficial trades, but also little time to correct mistakes. Zebra, valuing closure and predictability, will likely propose a small surplus trade that is almost surely acceptable to at least one counterpart, rather than a high-risk, high-gain offer. Gorilla will continue opportunistic cleanup trades, happy to accept any marginally positive swap and propose conservative offers that rely on others’ willingness to take slightly worse chips. Turtle now strongly prioritizes ending with extra BLUE and will see little reason to give it up; their final proposal will likely offer their remaining surplus RED for either BLUE or GREEN on terms that still leave them ahead. Rejections in this round often reflect tiny gains that responders deem not worth the risk or final-position anxiety, while accepted trades are ones that move players further toward their ideal mix (Zebra toward RED/GREEN, Gorilla toward BLUE/GREEN, Turtle toward BLUE).

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so green trades are zero-sum in value and mainly matter as a medium of exchange or to signal willingness to cooperate. Red and blue differ by player: cat values BLUE=0.70, RED=0.50; iguana values BLUE=0.30, RED=0.80; zebra values BLUE=0.80, RED=0.40. Efficiently, red should flow toward iguana and away from zebra, and blue should flow toward cat and zebra and away from iguana. Because players only know their own values, they must infer others’ valuations from observed trades and acceptance patterns. All three have relatively low ultimatum rejection thresholds and moderate dictator/trust tendencies, suggesting they are willing to accept small but positive gains, not just large windfalls. Proposers see an on-screen surplus calculator for their own payoffs but not others’, so proposals are anchored on self-gain and trial-and-error. Early in the game, proposals will likely be simple, conservative exchanges (e.g., 1-for-1 or 2-for-1 small bundles) aimed at finding counterparties without risking a loss. Over time, as they observe who accepts or rejects requests for specific colors, they update beliefs: iguana learns that others strongly want red or blue at particular rates, while cat and zebra infer that iguana values red highly and blue relatively poorly. Strategic uncertainty arises from the random selection among multiple acceptors, which slightly weakens bilateral targeting: a proposer cannot perfectly direct surplus to a specific partner when more than one is willing. Since there are only three rounds (nine total proposals), each player is incentivized to exploit clear arbitrage opportunities once discovered, but not to overreach with overly demanding trades that risk rejection and wasted turns. Given their profiles and the short horizon, most accepted trades will be modest positive-surplus exchanges for both sides, with a gradual pattern of red shifting toward iguana and blue shifting toward cat and zebra, mediated by green as a safe, symmetric unit of account.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no direct information about others’ private values. Each proposer is likely to test the market with simple, nearly symmetric offers that their surplus calculator shows as a small gain. Because everyone values green identically and moderately, early trades are likely to use green as a balancing chip or as a low-risk item to request. Given their low ultimatum thresholds and mixed trust tendencies, all three are inclined to accept any proposal that provides a clear positive value change, even if small. Round 0 thus functions as an exploratory stage where they probe others’ willingness to part with red or blue at specific rates.

By round 1, the history reveals that cat is willing to exchange green for blue and that iguana is willing to give up blue for red at relatively favorable terms to cat. This implies to observers that iguana values red more than blue and that cat values blue more than green. Zebra attempted to buy blue with green and was rejected, hinting that, at least for cat and iguana, green is not an attractive payment for small amounts of blue at a 2:1 green-to-blue rate. Proposers in this round adjust: cat now knows that buying red from iguana with blue is profitable, but must wait for the right chance because iguana just traded away some blue. Iguana may continue to convert blue into red, possibly targeting zebra now, while zebra should shift away from paying green for blue and instead try to monetize red, which appears relatively cheap for others but less valuable to zebra.

In the final round, holdings are more differentiated: iguana has accumulated red but given up meaningful amounts of blue and some green; cat has increased both red and blue at the cost of green; zebra has modestly shifted toward blue, given up some red, and traded green. Players now have stronger priors: iguana’s repeated willingness to pay for red confirms her high value for it; cat’s pattern shows a strong appetite for both red and blue when priced favorably; zebra’s final move in round 1 revealed that she is willing to trade red for blue at roughly 1:1, which is value-improving for her. With only three proposals left, each player focuses on clearly positive trades rather than experimenting. Cat will look for a last arbitrage between blue and red if possible; iguana may try to convert remaining blue and possibly some green into valuable red; zebra will likely accept any small improvement trade that increases blue or green if it does not cost too much red. Because of low rejection thresholds, modest ‘win–win’ proposals still have a good chance of acceptance, but any trade that is even slightly negative for the responder will be declined given the immediate feedback from the surplus calculator.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features heterogeneous private values on red and blue chips, with common-value green chips. All players start with 10 of each color and seek to maximize their own monetary payoff. Gorilla values red highly ($1.00), blue minimally ($0.10), and green at $0.50. Camel values blue moderately ($0.60), red low ($0.30), and green $0.50. Monkey values both red and blue highly ($1.00 and $0.90) and green $0.50. Strategic gains come from exchanging low-value colors for high-value ones, but players only know their own valuation structure. The UI surplus calculator means that any proposed trade that is a loss to a responder will almost always be rejected, while strictly profitable trades are likely to be accepted absent concerns about dynamic positioning. Because multiple acceptances trigger a random partner selection, proposers cannot finely target their most favorable counterpart: they must design offers that are acceptable to at least one counterpart and be aware that a surprisingly good deal for both camel and monkey might lead to a random match rather than the preferred one. Gorilla is moderately cooperative and not very rejection-prone, camel is self-protective and resource-guarding, and monkey is relatively prosocial but still payoff-focused. Across three short rounds with a fixed proposal order, we expect early exploratory offers, then convergence on clearly positive-sum, asymmetric trades: gorilla offloading blue for red from monkey, camel trying to turn green and red into blue, and monkey seeking to accumulate more red (from gorilla) and perhaps some blue (from camel) when the rate is favorable. Uncertainty focuses on who knows what: players observe past trades and can infer relative preferences (e.g., someone repeatedly offering blue likely values it low), but cannot see others’ dollar valuations directly. This can lead to cautious opening offers, some declined trades with overly one-sided rates, then more balanced and mutually profitable deals as patterns reveal partial information.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold 10 of each color and only know their own chip values. Gorilla is likely to tentatively probe for profitable exchanges of low-valued blue into higher-valued red from others, camel wants to cheaply acquire blue while shedding relatively low-valued red or common green, and monkey is willing to trade if the surplus is clear and may accept somewhat generous but not loss-making deals. With no trade history yet, early offers are exploratory and slightly conservative; players test counterpart willingness without giving away too much about their own valuation structure. Declines are quite possible in this round as people calibrate what ratios are acceptable.

After round 0, everyone has seen some willingness to trade at clearly positive-surplus terms. Gorilla has revealed a strong desire to acquire red and is now lighter on blue and green. Camel has signaled that blue is attractive enough to give up several green chips, implying blue is relatively valuable to him versus green. Monkey has shown readiness to accept an advantageous trade for red and blue but declined a green-for-red offer that wasn’t sufficiently profitable. In this round, players start updating beliefs: they infer that gorilla dislikes blue and values red, camel likes blue more than green, and monkey values red quite highly. We expect more targeted proposals: gorilla should focus on swapping remaining low-value blue for red or maybe green; camel will try to cheaply grow blue stock, now possibly using red instead of green; monkey may look to consolidate more red if he can do it without sacrificing too much blue or green.

Going into the final round, inventories are more differentiated: gorilla has offloaded much of her low-valued blue in exchange for high-valued red and some green; camel has accumulated blue by paying with green and red, but still wants to improve his mix if the price is right; monkey holds a strong bundle of red and blue but has given up some green. With only three proposal opportunities left, players focus on high-margin, last-chance trades rather than subtle signaling. Gorilla has little incentive to give up red or green unless she is receiving multiple units of a color she values much more (which she does not, since blue is low-value). Camel remains the most aggressive acquirer of blue, but he will not accept trades that reduce his dollar value. Monkey, being prosocial but payoff-conscious, may accept trades that slightly improve his surplus and help others if the effect is not costly. We expect at least one declined offer where someone overreaches on an exchange rate, and possibly one more mutually beneficial blue–red swap as players try to squeeze out final surplus.
