parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on exchanging privately valued red and blue chips against a common-value green currency, with all players starting from a symmetric 10-10-10 inventory but very different payoffs. Greens are always $0.50 to everyone, so they form a transparent, risk-free "money" that all players recognize as safe and fair. The real bargaining leverage lies in red and blue chips: each player privately sees that blues are quite valuable and reds are weaker, but the exact intensity differs. Otter values blue the highest at $1.00 and red at $0.40; tiger values blue at $0.90 and red at $0.70; alligator values blue at $0.60 and red at only $0.10. From an efficiency standpoint, the game pushes red chips away from alligator (who barely cares about them) toward tiger (who likes them most), and pushes blue chips away from alligator toward otter and tiger (who value them more). However, players do not see each other’s valuations; they only see outcomes of past trades and can use them to infer rough patterns. Alligator is older, fairly prosocial in prior tasks, and tolerant of inequality; he is likely to propose trades that clearly benefit him but also leave visible gains for at least one partner, using green as a neutral bridge. Otter shows extremely low minimum acceptable shares and very low dictator giving, but high empathy and strong preference for predictability; she is likely to accept almost any strictly beneficial trade, even small, and to propose safe, conservative offers that are clearly profitable for herself and at least not obviously bad for partners. Tiger appears prosocial and cognitively sharp, with a willingness to share in previous tasks; here he is likely to accept mutually beneficial trades and sometimes offer terms that leave surplus on the table to increase acceptance chances, especially when he can cheaply acquire red chips he values a lot. Strategic uncertainty comes from not knowing who values which colored chips most, nor how aggressively others will compete or undercut each other. Because multiple acceptors trigger a random tie-break, proposers cannot choose their trading partner, so they must design offers that still look acceptable to any likely responder. Over time, revealed acceptance behavior will hint that otter is hungry for blue, tiger for red and blue, and alligator is flexible as a seller of red. Given only three rounds and one offer per player per round, the bargaining path will be short: players will make reasonably simple, low-cognitive-load proposals that guarantee positive own-value gains and are not so lopsided that counterparts reject them, gradually moving chips toward higher-valuing owners while preserving individual payoff focus rather than maximizing group efficiency.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, all players hold 10 of each color and have no information about others’ private values. Green is obviously safe at $0.50, so early proposals are likely to use green as the currency side of trades. Alligator, who values red very little and blue modestly, is a natural early seller of red in exchange for green. Otter, with high blue value and moderate red value, will be cautious but eager to improve her mix as soon as a favorable trade appears. Tiger, with relatively high red and blue values, has a strong incentive to buy both colors cheaply if possible. In round 0, proposals will likely be exploratory and conservative: small quantities, clearly positive surplus for the proposer, designed so that any responder who values chips at least somewhat similarly will also see a gain. Because nobody yet knows who wants which color most, acceptance decisions rely entirely on the on-screen surplus calculator for one’s own side rather than deeper inference.

By the start of round 1, tiger has already executed two profitable trades and sits on more red and blue at the cost of some green, revealing that he is willing to pay green for both colored chips. Alligator has shown a willingness to sell red cheaply for green, hinting that his red valuation is low. Otter has signaled that she is willing to part with green for blue at favorable rates, so others might infer she values blue strongly. This emerging pattern suggests to all players that tiger likes red especially, otter likes blue, and alligator is the most flexible seller of red. Strategic dynamics now favor more targeted one-step improvements: alligator can again sell low-valued red for green or blue, otter can continue to buy blue if priced well, and tiger must be careful not to give up too many remaining greens, which anchor his safe value. The risk of overpaying in colored chips is still present because valuations remain private, but observed trades narrow the plausible ranges of others’ preferences.

Entering the final round, the key contours are clear: alligator has repeatedly sold red and now holds relatively more green and a modest amount of blue, consistent with treating red as low value. Otter has successfully accumulated some extra blue but has been more cautious in later moves after seeing both potential gains and failed offers. Tiger has bought considerable red and some blue while giving up many greens, aligning with his stronger preference for colored chips. Everyone now knows there are only three proposals left in the game, so there is no incentive to leave easy surplus on the table for future periods. Proposers in round 2 are likely to look for last, clean, one-step improvements in their own portfolios: alligator will try to exit remaining red or excess green on terms that strictly improve his own payoff; otter will selectively take any highly favorable chances to gain more blue or perhaps pick up cheap green if offered; tiger will accept only clearly profitable trades, particularly cheap red, but may avoid sacrificing scarce green. Because there is no continuation value, some marginally beneficial but very small gains may be ignored if they appear cognitively effortful or uncertain, but any visibly significant dollar gain highlighted by the calculator is likely to be accepted.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so it is the clear focal asset to accumulate. Private values for RED and BLUE differ: dolphin values both RED and BLUE at $0.10 (low), frog values RED at $0.90 and BLUE at $0.50 (high, especially RED), and pig values RED at $0.40 and BLUE at $0.30 (moderate). This creates natural trade directions: dolphin wants to dump RED/BLUE for GREEN; frog would like to acquire RED in particular and is roughly indifferent between BLUE and GREEN; pig gains from acquiring RED and BLUE when cheap enough while not overpaying with GREEN. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own RED/BLUE values, so they infer others’ values from observed willingness to trade. Since the experiment is three rounds with each of the three players proposing once per round, players must balance early aggressive trades (before rivals grab the easy gains) against the risk of offering too much surplus to others. Prosocial tendencies suggest dolphin and frog might be somewhat generous or tolerant of giving partners a share of surplus, while pig may be more opportunistic or quick to grab positive-value trades. Random recipient selection when multiple accept means proposers must anticipate competition for good offers and may, in later rounds, tailor terms that are clearly unattractive to one rival to target a preferred partner indirectly, even though they cannot choose the recipient directly. Overall, the likely pattern is early exploration with mild, mutually beneficial GREEN-for-RED/BLUE trades, followed by more targeted or picky proposals once players infer that RED is very valuable to frog and somewhat valuable to pig, and that dolphin strongly chases GREEN while being willing to part with RED/BLUE cheaply.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has symmetric chip counts but unknown private RED/BLUE values. Dolphin, valuing GREEN most, is likely to probe by trying to buy GREEN with her undervalued RED or BLUE, offering trades that are modestly favorable to potential partners. Frog, very prosocial and with a strong preference for RED, will likely test a trade that acquires RED using GREEN or BLUE, possibly offering near-fair or even slightly generous terms. Pig, more opportunistic, is inclined to accept any trade that clearly increases his own value and may propose simple value-improving swaps if he can see clear surplus, especially into RED. This first round should reveal initial willingness to trade GREEN versus RED/BLUE and start to teach players who seems hungry for which colors.

By round 1, some structure is visible from the first three turns. Dolphin has already signaled a willingness to give up multiple low-valued RED for GREEN and to sell RED to frog for GREEN, suggesting to both partners that dolphin values GREEN more than RED and is likely somewhat indifferent over RED/BLUE. Frog has already shown active interest in buying RED with GREEN, hinting that RED is important to her. Pig declined a BLUE-for-RED trade in round 0 that was only mildly positive to frog and would have been value-gaining for him if he valued RED highly; his decline suggests he is not desperate for RED at any price and is attentive to the exact surplus. In this round, dolphin is likely to refine offers toward frog or pig that secure more GREEN while still leaving counterparts with gains. Frog—strongly prosocial—may again propose RED-acquisition trades but now try to exploit dolphin’s eagerness for GREEN a bit more. Pig, seeing that both others want GREEN and that frog wants RED, may become choosier as a proposer, testing trades that upgrade his portfolio into RED/BLUE at favorable rates or simply abstaining from marginal offers if he expects better opportunities as a responder.

Entering the final round, players have clearer beliefs: dolphin is consistently trading away RED/BLUE for GREEN and seems comfortable giving partners some surplus; frog strongly accumulates RED and is willing to pay with GREEN or BLUE; pig is selective and has occasionally tested low-surplus offers that others have rejected. Everyone knows it is the last chance to adjust portfolios before payoffs, so proposals now prioritize clear private gains over information-gathering. Dolphin will likely continue to swap her cheap chips (RED/BLUE) into GREEN as long as she can still offer attractive terms—especially aiming proposals at frog, who seems less demanding. Frog, who now probably holds a good RED stock, may make one final attempt to refine her bundle—perhaps replacing some BLUE with more RED if possible, or she may pass on marginal trades that risk losing GREEN. Pig, recognizing that both partners regard GREEN as valuable, may either make a final attempt to upgrade into RED/BLUE at bargain rates or simply wait to accept any obviously positive final offers. Because it is the last round, all three are less inclined to offer very generous deals; instead, they look for trades that are strongly positive for themselves yet still just good enough that at least one partner will accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so green-to-green trades are pointless; only cross-color trades matter. The key private-value asymmetries are in RED and BLUE: iguana values RED $0.30 and BLUE $0.20; owl values RED $1.00 and BLUE $0.60; squid values RED $0.90 and BLUE $0.50. Everyone therefore strictly prefers RED over BLUE, and prefers GREEN over either blue or red at the margin, but the intensity of that preference differs. The main gains from trade come from moving RED from iguana (who values it least) to owl and squid (who value it most), and moving BLUE from owl and squid (who value it relatively more) toward iguana only if the exchange also helps them get more RED or keep high-value GREEN. However, because players know only their own valuations during play, they face uncertainty about how much the others are willing to pay in green or blue for red, and whether a rival will outbid them, triggering random recipient assignment. Personas matter: iguana is resource-protective and fairly calculating, so he is unlikely to offer large negative-surplus trades and will sometimes test for small, safe gains, preferring proposals that look at least modestly profitable. Owl is tolerant of unequal splits and not strongly punitive, so she is willing to accept small but positive gains, even if others might gain more. Squid is cooperative and empathetic, also inclined to accept modestly positive offers rather than hold out. The random-recipient rule when multiple acceptants exist discourages very generous offers that would attract both partners at once, because the sender cannot control who they actually trade with. Over three rounds, we therefore expect cautious, incremental proposals, especially early, with some offers failing when they are clearly negative-surplus for responders or when responders infer that better deals may come later. Successful trades will typically be simple one-for-one or small multi-chip exchanges of RED against GREEN or RED against BLUE that give each side a small positive expected value given their own valuations and limited beliefs about others.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, everyone still holds the symmetric 10-10-10 endowment, and nobody has observed others' willingness to trade. Proposers mainly test the waters with relatively simple, conservative offers that are weakly favorable from their own perspective and plausibly attractive to at least one counterpart. Because no player yet knows who values what, trades that look slightly lopsided in favor of responders are plausible as exploratory moves, but strongly negative-surplus offers for responders are unlikely to be accepted. The random-recipient rule also looms: any overly generous trade that would look good to both other players risks giving value to the 'wrong' recipient, so proposers tend to design deals that are attractive to one likely partner rather than both.

By the second round, one significant trade has occurred: owl has successfully bought RED from squid using GREEN. Public holdings reveal that squid was willing to part with RED for a relatively small green payment, signaling that squid cannot value RED dramatically more than GREEN. Iguana’s initial attempt to buy GREEN for RED failed, hinting that others might value GREEN at least as much as the red he offered. Proposers now adjust: owl, knowing she values RED very highly, continues to seek RED but with slightly better terms for the others; squid, having given up some RED, may explore rebuilding RED or converting surplus BLUE; iguana, after a failed first attempt, becomes more cautious, trying proposals that offer clearer gains to at least one partner while still improving his own mix toward higher personal value.

Entering the final round, players have updated beliefs from several accepted and rejected offers. Owl has been the most successful trader so far, steadily accumulating RED by paying GREEN and BLUE, consistent with her high private value for red. Squid has shown willingness to trade both RED and GREEN when terms are modestly favorable, while iguana has made one profitable but conservative trade. With only three proposal turns left, each player weighs the benefit of squeezing out one last marginal improvement against the risk that an aggressive ask will simply be declined. Final-round proposals therefore tend to be sharp: they offer clear immediate gains to at least one potential partner but may leave little surplus on the table, reflecting a last attempt to lock in value given observed acceptance behavior and the now-asymmetric chip inventories.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red and blue chips plus common-value green chips. Each player starts with an equal inventory (10 of each color) but very different marginal values: gorilla-purple-8501 (gorilla) values RED the highest (0.80) and BLUE moderately (0.50); llama-pink-7039 (llama) values BLUE the highest (0.80) and RED moderately (0.60); whale-orange-533 (whale) values BLUE low (0.30) and RED moderately (0.60). GREEN is worth 0.50 to everyone and thus acts like money or a numeraire. Efficient gains come mainly from trading red from llama/whale toward gorilla and blue from gorilla/whale toward llama, with green often serving as a balancing chip to compensate partners.

Key incentive patterns:
- Any bilateral trade that moves RED from llama or whale (0.60) to gorilla (0.80) increases total surplus by 0.20 per red chip moved. Gorilla is the natural red buyer.
- Any bilateral trade that moves BLUE from gorilla (0.50) or whale (0.30) to llama (0.80) increases total surplus by 0.30–0.50 per blue chip moved. Llama is the natural blue buyer, especially from whale.
- GREEN trades are zero-sum in value but can be used to compensate a partner who would otherwise lose value on red/blue moves.

Strategically, players only see their own values. Gorilla knows red is particularly valuable to her but does not know that both others also value red more than blue; she may infer that at least one other player values blue highly and seek blue-for-red trades. Llama knows blue is valuable to him and expects others to underprice it, making him a natural blue buyer asking for blue while paying with red or green. Whale, being prosocial and cognitively strong, is more willing to initiate and accept slightly unbalanced trades that create group surplus, but still sees his own payoff calculator and will avoid obvious losses.

Uncertainty arises from two main frictions: (1) incomplete information about others' valuations, which makes it risky to offer very generous terms because they might reveal weakness or leave value on the table; and (2) the rule that if multiple partners accept, the recipient is chosen randomly, so proposers must anticipate that any beneficial offer to multiple players could be randomly matched with whichever one accepts. As the game has only three rounds and each player proposes once per round, there is limited time to learn, so early offers tend to be safer, moderately surplus-positive trades that are clearly beneficial (or at least neutral) to the proposer and attractive to at least one partner.

Given the personas, gorilla is somewhat fairness-minded and risk-averse with high rejection tendencies; she is unlikely to give away surplus and will prefer trades that clearly improve or at least do not hurt her calculator. Llama is self-protective and less altruistic, with low rejection thresholds when responding, so he may accept small but positive gains and propose fairly one-sided trades when he can. Whale is the most prosocial and strategic; he is willing to propose and accept trades that share surplus, especially when they also improve overall efficiency, and he may act as a de facto facilitator who accepts slightly less favorable splits as long as he is not losing.

Across the three rounds, we should expect: (1) early, exploratory trades that test how attractive red-for-blue or red-for-green swaps are, with whale quite willing to accept profitable terms; (2) a focus on moving blue toward llama and red toward gorilla once players infer where their advantages lie, often using green to make offers mutually beneficial; and (3) incremental improvements rather than drastic full-portfolio restructuring, because of time constraints, limited strategic sophistication, and risk aversion. Some proposals will be declined because they accidentally impose a loss on both potential responders or are not quite attractive enough to compensate them, especially when proposed by llama or gorilla who are less inclined to give surplus away. Overall, the likely outcome is a modest reallocation of a few red and blue chips that slightly increases everyone’s payoffs, with whale and llama engaging in the most actual trades and gorilla being somewhat more conservative but still participating in at least one clearly beneficial exchange.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0 all players hold 10 of each chip color. No one knows others’ private values, so this round serves as exploratory bargaining. Gorilla proposes first and, given her high red value and moderate blue value, is likely to test a small trade that keeps her risk low while probing whether either partner will give up blue or green for red. Llama, valuing blue most, will then try to acquire blue cheaply, probably offering red or green. Whale, as the most prosocial and strategic, will look for trades that both make him a small gain and seem obviously beneficial to at least one partner, and he is comparatively more willing to accept offers that create mutual surplus. Because everyone is still uncertain about others’ valuations, terms will be relatively conservative, with small quantities and clearly positive calculator gains for both sides when trades succeed.

After round 0, gorilla observed that neither partner was eager to trade blue for red on a 1:1 basis, suggesting that red is not dramatically weaker than blue for them. Llama successfully bought blue from whale for green, learning that whale is willing to trade away low-value blue for common-value green; whale confirmed that such trades give him a small positive gain. No one has yet transacted with gorilla, which may make her more cautious but also more motivated to find a clearly beneficial deal. In round 1, proposals are likely to focus more on green as compensation, and players will refine their terms: llama may again target whale’s blue with a slightly improved or similarly favorable offer, while whale and gorilla experiment with red–green trades that leave partners at least weakly better off.

By the start of round 2, trade history has revealed partial structure: whale repeatedly accepted trades that offload his weaker blue chips for green or red, and both he and llama have engaged in mutually beneficial blue-related trades. Gorilla has finally completed a favorable red-for-green trade, confirming that at least one partner is willing to pay green for her valuable red. All players now know that: (1) blue is relatively cheap for whale, (2) green is a flexible bargaining chip, and (3) red has solid value for gorilla, as she does not part with it cheaply. With only three turns remaining in the game, proposals will be more targeted and slightly bolder but still conservative, as each player wants to secure one last positive trade without risking a loss. Gorilla may again try modest red-for-green or red-for-blue offers that guarantee nonnegative gains; llama will look for one more blue acquisition from whale if terms permit; and whale, already having improved his position, will only accept clearly profitable offers and may float one last trial trade that is efficient but could be rejected if perceived as too thin by partners.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip-bargaining game features individual private values for red and blue chips, with green chips worth a common $0.50 to everyone. Each player starts with 10 of each color. Strategically, red is almost always high value (around $0.90–$1) and blue is more heterogeneous (from $0.30 to $1). Because everyone knows the public value ranges but not each other’s exact private values, they can infer that red is likely relatively valuable to all, while blue might be more or less valuable depending on the person. However, in the actual play, each person only sees their own dollar values and must infer others’ preferences from what they propose and accept.

Given the concrete values:
- Duck: RED $1.00, BLUE $1.00, GREEN $0.50 (values red and blue equally, both high)
- Lion: RED $0.90, BLUE $0.50, GREEN $0.50 (stronger preference for red, indifferent between blue and green)
- Tiger: RED $1.00, BLUE $0.30, GREEN $0.50 (strong preference for red, blue is weak)

The main surplus opportunities arise when a player who values a chip highly can get it from someone who values it less, without the counterparty losing much or at all. Here, blue is especially cheap for Tiger and medium for Lion, but very valuable for Duck; red is highly valued by Duck and Tiger and almost as high for Lion; green is common value and sits in the middle.

Duck’s best deals involve acquiring more chips of either red or blue without giving up much in expected value. Because Duck values both red and blue at $1, they are pretty symmetric from Duck’s perspective; Duck cares more about gaining quantity of any high-value color than about which one it is. Lion and Tiger both have an incentive to sell blue (especially Tiger, for whom blue is just $0.30) if they can receive red or sometimes green in return. Because of the random-recipient rule when multiple players accept, proposers must worry about who might accept their offer, as the realized partner can change the trade’s actual surplus distribution.

Personality-wise, Duck is resource-protective but numerate and has high tolerance for unequal outcomes; they can be generous in structured situations but will not give away surplus in a zero-sum trade. Lion is moderately generous but has relatively strict fairness standards for their own payoffs and may reject offers that look too one-sided. Tiger is relatively trusting and numerate but not particularly altruistic; he will pursue good-value trades but will not give up gains just to help others.

We should see early rounds used to test what others value: modest, slightly favorable offers that leave clear gains for counterparts but are still positive for the proposer. Once responders reveal their acceptance thresholds, later rounds can tighten margins. Because the game is only three rounds long and each round has exactly one proposal per person, players face pressure to capture beneficial exchanges quickly but still avoid obvious losses shown by the surplus calculator. Uncertainty about others’ private values and about simultaneous responses also means that some profitable trades fail if players worry that the proposer is absorbing too much of the surplus, or if two counterparts both accept and risk being randomly chosen when their gain is smaller than the other’s. Overall, the likely pattern is a few mutually beneficial trades concentrating blue into Duck and red into Lion or Tiger, with green occasionally used as a neutral exchange medium when players want to avoid giving up their high-value colors.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players still have symmetric inventories (10 of each color) and only know their own chip values. The first proposals are likely to be exploratory, probing which colors others seem willing to give up. Duck has strong value for both red and blue, so she is cautious about giving either away too cheaply. Lion and Tiger suspect red is broadly valuable and will be reluctant to trade it away unless incentivized. Because nobody has yet learned about others’ preferences from actual trades, offers in this round are likely to be simple and slightly favorable for the proposer, but still leaving obvious, non-trivial gains to entice at least one responder to accept.

After round 0, the history reveals that Tiger was willing to sell blue for green and red and twice accepted deals that improved his value, especially when he offloaded low-value blue. Lion gave up some blue to get red, signaling that he values red more than blue. Duck traded away some green to gain blue, suggesting that she puts at least moderate value on blue relative to green. With these signals, players start round 1 with better beliefs: Tiger is the cheapest blue holder and likes red; Lion wants red and is OK parting with blue; Duck likes blue enough to pay with green. Proposals now focus more clearly on exploiting these revealed comparative advantages, aiming for narrower, more efficient surplus splits.

By round 2, patterns are clear: Tiger reliably tries to convert blue into either red or green; Lion keeps pushing to increase his red stock while using blue and green as currency; Duck is willing to part with green, and occasionally red when the calculator shows a solid gain, in order to accumulate more high-value chips. Inventories have diverged enough that marginal valuations over quantities may tilt slightly toward favoring diversification or fine-tuning rather than making large exchanges. With only one final proposal each, players now look for any remaining clearly positive trades, but they no longer need big moves; instead, they may test small, safe offers. Some of these attempts will be declined if they leave too little surplus for responders, who care about their own payoff and face no future rounds to reward cooperation.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game centers on exchanging privately valued red and blue chips plus common-value green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color, but values differ: Bear highly values blue ($1.00) and dislikes red ($0.20), with neutral green ($0.50); Goose highly values both red and blue ($0.80 each), with neutral green; Ram moderately values red ($0.50) and blue ($0.80), with neutral green.

Individually optimal trades convert low-value chips into high-value ones. At the social level, the biggest surplus comes from moving red away from Bear (who values it least) toward Goose (who values it most), and to a lesser extent from Bear and Goose sending blue to Bear (who values it more) only if they get strong compensation. Green is a stable medium of exchange because it is worth $0.50 to everyone; participants can safely use green to compensate each other without worrying about hidden value asymmetries.

Strategic uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own private valuations, not others'. They see ranges suggesting red and blue might be valuable but do not know exactly how. This makes them cautious about giving up too many red or blue chips: they fear overpaying in terms of foregone value if those chips are worth more than they think to others. The UI surplus calculator removes calculation mistakes for one’s own side of any proposed trade but does not reveal counterpart values.

Personas matter for style: Bear is older, moderately prosocial, and tolerant of low payoffs, so he is willing to propose and accept modest but safe gains, especially in blue-for-red trades. Goose and Ram are trusting and tolerant of inequality, suggesting relatively low thresholds for accepting small but positive gains, especially early in the game when risk of regret is lower. All three are patient and not strongly rejection-oriented, so they are inclined to accept positive-surplus trades even if they suspect the proposer might be doing slightly better.

Because only one of multiple accepting responders is randomly selected, senders cannot target a specific partner. This pushes senders to design deals that are attractive to either counterparty and not so generous that either response would be bad. Over three rounds (nine total proposals), players can gradually reveal information about their own preferences through which offers they post and which they accept. However, in a single short game, we expect only limited learning: players tend to make straightforward, locally improving trades (convert obviously low-value chips to higher-value ones at favorable terms) rather than subtly probing others’ valuations.

Given these incentives, we forecast early trades that move red away from Bear in exchange for green or blue, and modest blue-for-red trades between Bear and the others. Goose and Ram, being more trusting and inequality-tolerant, will accept many offers with small but clear personal gains. Over time, inventory constraints (e.g., running low on red or green) slightly dampen further trades but do not eliminate profitable options. The likely path shows a few accepted trades each round with Bear gradually offloading red and acquiring blue or green, Goose accumulating red where cheap, and Ram flexibly arbitraging between them when a clear surplus gain is available.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip color. No one knows others’ private values, so initial proposals emphasize safe, clearly beneficial trades from the proposer’s perspective, often using common-value green as currency. Bear is especially eager to shed low-value red for anything better, while Goose and Ram are open to modest gains and unlikely to reject offers that give them a clear positive surplus. With inventories full and no history yet, risk of over-trading is low, so acceptance thresholds are mild.

By round 1, some information has emerged: Bear has signaled he wants blue and is willing to part with red and later with green; Goose has shown a willingness to trade green for red; Ram has used green to buy blue from Bear. Inventories now differ, but everyone still has sufficient chips to engage in new trades. Players update beliefs slightly: they infer that others also value blue and red reasonably highly (since they were willing to trade green for them) but still do not know exact values. Strategic focus shifts to further improving portfolios: Bear continues to unload red when possible and accumulate blue; Goose opportunistically buys more red at near-even green cost; Ram remains a flexible trader, converting green into whichever color yields net surplus. Because accepted trades in round 0 were clearly beneficial for both sides, all three remain open to additional modest-gain exchanges.

Going into the final round, inventories have shifted considerably, but each player still holds multiple chips of each color. Patterns are clearer: Bear repeatedly offers 3 red for 2 blue, revealing his strong preference for blue over red; Goose repeatedly uses green to acquire red, revealing her high red valuation; Ram uses green to buy both blue and red when favorable. Because time is about to end, players are more willing to accept any remaining positive-surplus deals, even if incremental. No one wants to leave obvious gains unrealized. However, they avoid trades that turn green into strictly lower-value bundles (e.g., Ram will not overpay green for red once his marginal value edge is thin). Final trades in this round are thus still mutually beneficial but may be slightly less generous than earlier ones as players push closer to their own value ratios.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on exchanging red and blue chips, which have different private values for each player, while green chips are common-value and relatively unattractive to move since everyone values them the same ($0.50). All begin with 10 of each chip. Ram values BLUE the highest at $1.00 and RED at $0.30; Lion values BLUE at $0.60 and RED at $0.50; Octopus values BLUE at only $0.10 and RED at $0.20. This creates clear gains-from-trade opportunities: Octopus is the low-value holder of BLUE and mid-value holder of RED, while Ram is the very high-value holder of BLUE and low-value holder of RED, so trades where Octopus gives BLUE to Ram in exchange for RED are especially surplus-generating. Lion sits in between, with moderate values for both colors, and can benefit by selling BLUE to Ram or buying cheap BLUE from Octopus, depending on terms. However, each player knows only their own valuations, so they must infer others’ values and willingness to trade from observed offers and acceptances. Social preference priors matter at the margin: Lion and Octopus are relatively prosocial and tolerant of unequal splits, so they may propose trades that leave others visibly better off or accept modest personal gains, while Ram is more self-protective and will demand clearly positive personal returns and may decline trades with only small or ambiguous benefits. Strategic uncertainty arises because proposals are bilateral in structure but multilateral in response: any non-sender can accept, and when more than one does, the partner is selected at random. This means a sender cannot precisely target a single counterpart and must design offers attractive enough to at least one specific player but possibly also to the other; yet, with private values, they can only guess which side gains more. Over three rounds (nine total proposal turns), players are likely to explore price-like exchange rates early, updating based on who accepts. As they learn, they can tailor more directed offers (e.g., Octopus systematically offloading BLUE to whoever seems to value it more, likely Ram), while using green sparingly, since trading green does not create differential surplus. Because all have calculators showing their own net change from each proposed offer, acceptance decisions will be individually rational: any accepted trade yields a weakly positive monetary gain for the accepting player, but the surplus division and learning externalities drive the richer dynamics.

rounds.round_explanation: In Round 0, everyone still has symmetric inventories and no information about others’ chip values beyond public ranges. Proposals are exploratory: players test which colors others might be willing to give up. Prosocial Lion and Octopus are willing to suggest offers that plausibly help others, while self-protective Ram waits for clear bargains. Because no one yet knows who values BLUE versus RED more, offers will likely be modest in size and somewhat symmetric in appearance (e.g., similar numbers of chips exchanged) to probe counterpart willingness without risking large inventory shifts.

By Round 1, Ram has successfully acquired some RED from Octopus in exchange for BLUE, revealing to both that Octopus is willing to shed BLUE and values it relatively little, while Ram is happy to take it. Lion, seeing Ram’s acceptance of a BLUE-for-RED style trade, can infer that Ram probably values BLUE more and RED less than typical, but the exact valuations remain unknown. Inventory positions are now slightly asymmetric: Octopus is down BLUE and up RED; Ram is up BLUE and down RED; Lion remains at baseline. Players start to design offers more pointedly: Octopus will likely continue to offload BLUE for RED, targeting Ram, while Lion may tentatively try selling BLUE to Ram or buying cheap BLUE from Octopus. Ram, after an aggressive but declined offer, will adjust terms to give clearer gains to others while preserving large gains for himself.

Entering Round 2, the pattern is clearer: Octopus has repeatedly offered BLUE for RED, and Ram has taken those trades, accumulating more BLUE and giving up RED. This strongly signals to both Lion and Octopus that Ram’s BLUE valuation is high and his RED valuation is low, while Octopus’ behavior signals that he is relatively eager to offload BLUE. Lion has remained cautious, not having found a clearly favorable trade. With only one round left, Octopus may make one more BLUE-for-RED attempt but adjust rates to ensure Ram still benefits; Ram, now holding many BLUE chips, becomes choosier, accepting only trades that noticeably improve his value. Lion, recognizing she might be missing gains, may finally propose a modest trade to engage Ram, but given uncertainty and Ram’s tough stance, her offer still risks being declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50, so everyone is happy to trade into GREEN if they can get it cheaply, but nobody will sell GREEN away unless they are compensated by gaining higher-valued private chips. The core private-value asymmetries are: buffalo (RED=$0.90, BLUE=$0.10), cat (RED=$0.40, BLUE=$0.20), and duck (RED=$0.40, BLUE=$0.90). This creates clean directional incentives: buffalo wants RED and is almost indifferent or slightly negative on BLUE; duck wants BLUE and is almost indifferent on RED; cat is relatively low on both RED and BLUE and values GREEN the same as others. The efficient pattern would be for buffalo to accumulate RED from cat and duck, and for duck to accumulate BLUE from buffalo and cat, with cat giving up some RED/BLUE in exchange for extra GREEN or side-payments in the other chip she slightly prefers. However, players only know their own valuations and see only that all chips are publicly within $0.10–$1.00 for RED/BLUE, and fixed at $0.50 for GREEN. They also face random partner selection whenever more than one responder accepts, which discourages extremely generous, multi-beneficiary trades. Given the personas, buffalo and duck are both relatively tolerant of unequal outcomes and not strongly punitive, making them willing to accept even modestly positive or slightly lopsided trades. Cat is more guarded and less trusting, with medium minimum acceptable payoff behavior, so she tends to reject offers that give her only very small gains, and to structure her own offers to capture decent surplus. Over three short rounds, each player gets three proposal turns. Early proposals are likely exploratory and conservative, often involving small quantities and emphasizing GREEN as a safe numeraire anchor. As history unfolds, each player infers who might value which colors more by watching which offers get accepted. Because the game is short and random tie-breaking over multiple accepters can dilute targeted reciprocity, we expect mostly bilateral-looking offers that are clearly attractive to just one counterpart at a time, with each proposer making sure the calculator shows them a positive surplus. The forecast below constructs one plausible full path where players gradually gravitate toward buffalo-RED and duck-BLUE specialization, while cat ends up with relatively more GREEN and modest amounts of the other chips, reflecting cautious but opportunistic trading.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, no one knows others’ private values, but everyone knows the public GREEN=$0.50. A natural safe strategy is to propose trades that are clearly favorable to the proposer in their own calculator, while still being plausibly attractive to at least one counterpart given the public ranges. Buffalo, who likes RED a lot and BLUE very little, will tentatively try to convert some BLUE into RED or GREEN. Cat, more cautious, will likely seek GREEN while giving up some RED or BLUE she doesn’t love, but will avoid big concessions. Duck, who highly values BLUE, will probe for BLUE-acquiring trades that look cheap and might test whether anyone is willing to sell BLUE for modest compensation. With no prior trade data, all three will keep volumes small and look for any acceptance as a signal about others’ preferences.

By the second round, players have seen that buffalo was eager to accept GREEN for RED (suggesting buffalo values RED more than GREEN and is not desperate to dump RED), that cat was willing to trade away RED for GREEN but then traded GREEN away for BLUE (hinting she doesn’t treat GREEN as sacred and that BLUE has at least some value), and that duck was willing to spend GREEN to get BLUE, strongly suggesting that duck values BLUE highly. These observations push buffalo toward targeting duck as a likely buyer of BLUE, and targeting cat for RED trades. Cat has learned that both others will accept trades that give them clear directional gains (buffalo into RED, duck into BLUE). Duck infers that cat will sell GREEN for BLUE and that buffalo is a strong RED buyer. With only two rounds left, proposers are slightly bolder in quantities, aiming to lock in their preferred inventories.

Going into the final round, everyone has clearer qualitative beliefs: buffalo has repeatedly shown willingness to gain RED even when giving up GREEN or BLUE; duck has openly paid GREEN and then BLUE for more BLUE and some RED; cat has acted as a flexible middle player, swapping RED for GREEN, then GREEN for BLUE, then more GREEN for BLUE. Final-round incentives are to refine portfolios: buffalo will try to convert low-valued BLUE into RED or GREEN if possible; cat may accept a last small gain if it does not expose her to big downside; duck will pay modestly for extra BLUE or shed some surplus RED if it yields any clear benefit. Because there is no continuation value beyond this round, proposers are willing to accept any trade giving them even a small positive gain, while responders reject anything non-positive. However, with chip inventories already skewed by previous trades, not every desired swap is feasible or mutually beneficial, so one or more final proposals may be declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with 10 chips of each color (RED, BLUE, GREEN), but RED and BLUE are privately valued and strongly asymmetric, while GREEN is a common-value chip worth $0.50 each to everyone. The key tensions are: (1) GREEN is moderately valuable and symmetric, so there is no obvious gain from trading GREEN for GREEN; (2) RED and BLUE are highly misaligned across players, so bilateral trades in those colors can generate surplus; and (3) all trades are one-shot proposals with simultaneous accept/decline responses and random matching if multiple players accept, creating strategic uncertainty about competition over attractive deals.

Private values:
- butterfly-blue-7661: RED $0.30, BLUE $0.10, GREEN $0.50
- kangaroo-blue-9562: RED $0.70, BLUE $0.20, GREEN $0.50
- squid-purple-3630: RED $0.40, BLUE $0.80, GREEN $0.50

Surplus patterns:
- kangaroo values RED the most ($0.70) and BLUE the least among others ($0.20). She wants to accumulate RED and shed BLUE when possible.
- squid values BLUE very highly ($0.80) and RED moderately ($0.40), so squid is especially eager to buy BLUE and is less excited about giving it up. Squid can sell some RED if compensated with BLUE or GREEN.
- butterfly values both RED and BLUE relatively low, but slightly prefers RED ($0.30) to BLUE ($0.10). Butterfly can profit from selling BLUE to squid and RED to kangaroo if she can get GREEN or a favorable exchange ratio.

Given personas, kangaroo is strongly self-protective and unlikely to give away value; she will propose aggressive, self-favoring trades and will only accept high private gains. Squid is more prosocial and trusting, willing to initiate cooperative-seeming deals that still benefit her, and more willing to accept moderate gains. Butterfly is tolerant of unequal outcomes, relatively cooperative, and dislikes uncertainty; she is likely to make near-fair or slightly self-favorable offers that she believes others might accept. The surplus calculator means each player always knows the exact private gain or loss from any trade on offer, so acceptance decisions will be tightly tied to private value differences rather than mistakes.

Across three rounds (nine total proposal turns), players will explore a few salient directions: (a) butterfly offering RED or BLUE for GREEN, trying to convert low-value chips into higher-value GREEN; (b) kangaroo trying to buy RED with BLUE or occasionally GREEN; (c) squid trying to buy BLUE with RED or GREEN. Uncertainty arises because proposers do not know others' exact values or how strict they are in accepting gains. They must infer, from acceptances and rejections, which counterpart values which chips and how intensely, and adjust later proposals under time pressure.

Random selection when multiple players accept creates extra risk: if a deal is obviously generous to recipients, more than one may accept, and the proposer cannot choose the best counterpart. This should make proposers avoid making trades that are simultaneously very attractive to both potential recipients unless they are indifferent across who they trade with. Because there are only three rounds, players have limited time to learn each other's valuations, so behavior will be somewhat exploratory at first (round 0) and then more targeted once they see which colors other players respond to.

Overall, expect early small, almost risk-free surplus trades as players probe each other's preferences (especially butterfly and squid), then somewhat more aggressive attempts by kangaroo and squid to convert low-value chips into high-value ones in later rounds, constrained by others' willingness to accept and the fear of overpaying when chip inventories are still large.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 chips of each color. No one has yet observed what others value, so proposals are exploratory. Butterfly, being relatively cooperative and tolerant of unequal splits, likely starts with modest offers that are at least slightly beneficial to her while plausibly acceptable to someone else. Kangaroo, very self-protective, will test whether she can cheaply acquire her favorite chip (RED) using chips she values less. Squid, prosocial and strategically aware, will likely try to buy BLUE, her highest-valued chip, but not at a large premium because she knows nothing about others' willingness to trade.

After round 0, the observed trades reveal partial information. Butterfly sold BLUE for GREEN to kangaroo, indicating that at least one counterpart is willing to pay 2 BLUE for 1 GREEN. Kangaroo then traded 3 BLUE for 2 RED with squid, showing that squid values BLUE enough to give up RED, and that kangaroo strongly pursues RED. Squid’s declined GREEN-for-BLUE offer revealed that neither butterfly nor kangaroo is willing to trade away BLUE at 1:1 for GREEN given their private values. Now inventories are slightly shifted: butterfly has fewer BLUE and more GREEN; kangaroo has gained some BLUE from butterfly then converted BLUE into RED, and squid has increased BLUE and reduced RED. In round 1, butterfly can refine offers, possibly selling RED (which she values modestly) to kangaroo for GREEN. Kangaroo, seeing squid’s appetite for BLUE, is likely to continue offering BLUE for RED. Squid, having her strong BLUE demand partially satisfied, may now try to convert excess RED or GREEN into more BLUE or modestly rebalance if she believes she can still profit.

By round 2, trading patterns are clearer: kangaroo consistently pays BLUE for RED, revealing a high marginal value for RED and relatively low value for BLUE. Squid repeatedly accepts these trades, confirming BLUE’s high value to her and moderate value for RED. Butterfly has twice sold chips (first BLUE, then RED) to kangaroo in exchange for GREEN, suggesting she’s content converting specific-color chips into common-value GREEN at roughly break-even or small-gain rates. Inventories now reflect these trades: butterfly has fewer RED and BLUE, more GREEN; kangaroo holds more RED and slightly less BLUE and GREEN; squid holds more BLUE and fewer RED. Knowing this is the final round, each proposer has one last chance. Butterfly may try a slightly more favorable GREEN-for-RED or GREEN-for-BLUE offer to kangaroo, kangaroo may push one more BLUE-for-RED exchange, and squid may try a final opportunistic proposal, but all are constrained by available chips and by how strict others have appeared in earlier acceptance behavior.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with symmetric chip inventories (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but asymmetric values for RED and BLUE. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so the only potential gains from trade come from reallocating RED and BLUE across players according to their private values.

Private per-chip values:
- bat-pink-1751 (bat): RED $0.20, BLUE $0.60, GREEN $0.50
- fox-green-2854 (fox): RED $0.60, BLUE $1.00, GREEN $0.50
- frog-blue-6296 (frog): RED $0.70, BLUE $0.30, GREEN $0.50

Implied comparative advantages:
- bat: Likes BLUE more than RED (0.60 vs 0.20) and is neutral on GREEN vs BLUE (0.50 vs 0.60). Bat is happy to trade away RED for BLUE or GREEN if they do not sacrifice too much value.
- fox: Values BLUE the most (1.00), then RED (0.60), then GREEN (0.50). Fox wants to accumulate BLUE and is willing to give up GREEN and, to a lesser extent, RED to obtain BLUE at a favorable rate.
- frog: Values RED the most (0.70), then GREEN (0.50), then BLUE (0.30). Frog wants to accumulate RED and is willing to part with BLUE and possibly GREEN.

Socially efficient direction of trade:
- Move RED from bat (0.20) and fox (0.60) toward frog (0.70).
- Move BLUE from frog (0.30) and bat (0.60) toward fox (1.00).
- GREEN is common value and mainly used as a flexible medium of exchange if players mis-estimate others’ values.

Because players only know their own values but see everyone’s inventories and the trade history, they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. The UI surplus calculator ensures that each acceptance decision is individually rational relative to a player’s own values: no one will knowingly accept a trade that leaves them strictly worse off in dollar terms. Proposers can easily see whether a tentative proposal is personally profitable, but they must guess whether at least one counterparty will also see it as profitable.

Given their prosocial and low-rejection personas, all three players are relatively willing to accept small positive gains and do not strongly punish unequal splits. At the same time, all are numerate enough to avoid obvious losses. Hence we should expect mild-but-not-extreme generosity in proposals: trades that give counterparties noticeable but not huge gains, especially early on. As the finite horizon (three rounds, nine total proposal turns) approaches its end, players with high-value inventories (fox’s BLUE, frog’s RED) are less eager to give up their best chips without clear compensation.

Key uncertainty and strategic tensions:
- Hidden valuations: No one knows that fox values BLUE at 1.00 or that frog values RED at 0.70; proposers must infer this gradually.
- Random recipient when multiple accept: This discourages highly targeted deals meant for a specific counterparty, pushing players to structure trades that at least two parties might find acceptable or to design trades clearly attractive to only one of them.
- Inventory constraints: Over multiple accepted trades, some players may deplete their low-value chips and then have to consider offering more valuable ones, which changes their bargaining stance across rounds.
- Time pressure: With only three rounds, each player has just three chances to be proposer. They may front-load more exploratory, relatively generous offers early, then become more selective later if they have already improved their portfolios.

In this forecast, bat tends to propose somewhat exploratory and prosocial trades that try to convert RED into GREEN or BLUE, fox focuses on accumulating BLUE while occasionally experimenting with trades for RED to probe frog’s preferences, and frog prioritizes accumulating RED while using BLUE as the main currency to pay for it. Across the nine turns, we see a few mutually beneficial accepted trades (especially involving frog paying BLUE for RED) and several declined proposals where the proposer misjudges how much value the counterparties place on particular colors.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players have identical inventories (10 of each color) and no information about others’ private values beyond the public common-value range hints. They are likely to use the first few proposals as probes: offering trades that yield them a clear personal gain while leaving at least one counterparty better off under reasonable value assumptions (e.g., someone likes RED more than they do, or someone is willing to give up GREEN or BLUE at modest rates). Because everyone is prosocial and tolerant of unequal splits, modest but clear win–win trades are likely to be accepted, particularly if structured so that at least one counterparty gains a few cents per chip. We should expect at least one accepted trade in this first round, with frog especially willing to buy RED using BLUE, since that is privately very attractive for them.

By round 1, a key signal has emerged: frog successfully traded BLUE for RED with bat. Bat learns that frog is at least somewhat willing to pay BLUE for RED, suggesting frog values RED more than BLUE. Frog confirms their own advantage by gaining RED at a favorable internal rate. Fox observes that neither earlier RED-for-GREEN nor RED-for-BLUE trades cleared, while frog’s BLUE-for-RED offer did, which hints that bat may value BLUE more than RED, and frog may value RED strongly. With these inferences, proposals are more likely to target frog as the natural RED-buyer and fox as the natural BLUE-buyer. However, all players are still unsure of the exact valuations, so some proposals may be slightly too aggressive (demanding too many high-value chips) and get declined. We should see at least one more RED-for-BLUE trade executed, probably with frog buying additional RED using BLUE.

Entering the final round, some reallocation has already taken place: frog has increased their RED holdings and reduced BLUE, while bat and fox each have slightly more BLUE and somewhat less RED. These patterns reinforce others’ beliefs that frog is the main RED-buyer and that BLUE is relatively more valuable to bat and fox. With only one proposal opportunity left for each player, they become more selective: they will only propose trades that clearly increase their own payoff and are plausibly attractive to at least one counterparty given the history. The most natural remaining gains involve frog trying to buy another small batch of RED using BLUE or possibly GREEN, and bat or fox occasionally probing small-margin trades that do not risk their now more valuable inventories. Because of the tight horizon and the fact that some of the most obvious gains from trade have already been realized, we expect at most one additional small accepted trade in this round, and multiple cautious offers that get declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game features a symmetric endowment (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN each) but asymmetric private values for RED and BLUE. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so it serves as a natural, low-friction medium of exchange. dog-green-8079 (Dog) and turtle-red-9966 (Turtle) both value BLUE highly ($1.00) and RED cheaply ($0.50), while parrot-pink-5400 (Parrot) values RED slightly more than BLUE (RED $0.60, BLUE $0.30). Thus, Dog and Turtle want to convert excess RED into BLUE, while Parrot wants to convert BLUE into RED. Absent full information, each only knows their own valuations, so they infer others’ values from observed proposals and acceptances. Incentive-wise, any trade that increases a player’s total dollar value is attractive, but players may avoid proposals that appear too generous relative to their own surplus, especially under limited rounds. Because all responders see their own net gain/loss from a posted trade, acceptance decisions tend to be myopic and value-based rather than strategic signaling. Random assignment of the recipient when multiple players accept creates uncertainty: a proposer may target a type (e.g., the high-BLUE type) but cannot control which accepting partner they get. Over three rounds, the likely dynamic is that Dog and Turtle try to offload RED and possibly GREEN for BLUE, testing how much surplus they must share, while Parrot, somewhat prosocial but also uncertainty-averse, trades away relatively low-value BLUE if they can secure RED at a modest GREEN or BLUE cost. Behavioral priors suggest Dog is tolerant of unequal outcomes and somewhat flexible, Parrot is fairly generous but dislikes unpredictability, and Turtle is more cautious and prefers structured, clearly beneficial trades, which nudges the game toward simple, obviously Pareto-improving exchanges using GREEN as the balancing chip color. We forecast a path where early trades reveal that at least one other player strongly values BLUE, Parrot capitalizes by selling RED-friendly bundles, and Dog and Turtle end with more BLUE than they started, while Parrot ends with more RED and roughly similar or slightly improved total value, with some potential surplus left on the table due to the short horizon and incomplete information.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has 10 of each chip. No one has seen any proposals yet, so beliefs about others’ values are uninformative. Dog and Turtle privately know that BLUE is very valuable and RED is cheap for them, while Parrot knows that RED is their best chip and BLUE is relatively weak. Early on, players are likely to experiment with simple, clearly advantageous trades that test demand for RED and BLUE, probably using GREEN as a neutral payment token since all value it equally. Because there are three full rounds, players can afford some exploration now, but they still prefer trades that are obviously positive in their own calculator. We forecast one early, generous but slightly inefficient trade that reveals Parrot’s RED preference to at least one of the high-BLUE players, setting up more focused proposals later.

By round 1, the history shows that Parrot twice accepted trades where they gave up BLUE in exchange for RED, implying to Dog and Turtle that Parrot likely values RED relatively highly and BLUE relatively weakly. Dog and Turtle now each hold slightly fewer RED and slightly more BLUE, while Parrot has accumulated extra RED and given up some BLUE, with GREEN unchanged so far. This information encourages Dog and Turtle to continue offering RED-for-BLUE packages to Parrot, but Parrot, having already shifted their portfolio toward RED, may become more selective and insist on better terms or on receiving GREEN instead of RED. Proposers face a trade-off between offering generous ratios that Parrot will clearly accept and preserving more of the BLUE surplus for themselves. GREEN is now a more salient balancing tool for Parrot, who has many REDs and may not want to take more RED without an offsetting gain.

Entering the final round, players have observed repeated patterns: Parrot is the main counterparty willing to give up BLUE for RED and sometimes for GREEN, while Dog and Turtle are reluctant to part with GREEN and focused on acquiring BLUE or occasionally GREEN when it is cheap. Because this is the last opportunity, proposals will be more targeted and less experimental. Dog and Turtle, both high-BLUE types, will only accept trades that yield immediate positive value (or at least break-even if they misjudge counterpart values), and Parrot, now holding a substantial RED pile, is mainly looking to convert excess RED into GREEN or modest amounts of BLUE on favorable terms. The remaining uncertainty is whether any player overestimates others’ willingness to trade GREEN or BLUE at thin margins; this can lead to declined offers that would have been mutually beneficial with finer-tuned ratios. With only three turns left, each proposer is likely to favor simple, one-step improvements to their own portfolio, even if that leaves some efficiency gains unrealized.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. GREEN is a common-value chip at $0.50 each for everyone. BLUE is low-value ($0.10) for all three players. RED is $0.30 for Badger and Peacock but $0.50 for Unicorn, so Unicorn uniquely values RED more and has the strongest incentive to acquire RED from the others. Because BLUE is low for everyone, it is the natural medium of exchange: people are generally willing to give up BLUE to acquire either GREEN (high, common) or—if they realize it—RED when they suspect others may value it less. However, players only know their own private values and see only coarse public ranges for others, so Badger and Peacock do not know that Unicorn’s RED is strictly more valuable than theirs; Unicorn, in contrast, can infer that RED might be more variable and that he might benefit from accumulating it. This creates asymmetric-information-driven bargaining: Unicorn is willing to overpay in BLUE for RED if others will accept, while Badger and Peacock seek GREEN and will not knowingly sacrifice their own surplus. Given the personas, Peacock is relatively prosocial and cognitively sharp, likely to search for mutually beneficial GREEN-for-BLUE or GREEN-for-RED trades and to respond positively to small, clearly positive surplus offers. Badger is cooperative but more uncertainty-averse and lower in reflective reasoning, so she prefers simple, mildly profitable trades and avoids riskier proposals that could be rejected. Unicorn is comfortable with unequal splits and aims to exploit undervalued RED, focusing less on fairness and more on cheap accumulation of RED while dumping BLUE. Across three short rounds, the most likely pattern is a few modest, clearly win–win trades: Unicorn buying RED from Badger or Peacock with BLUE, and possibly Peacock or Badger exchanging BLUE for GREEN. Some promising trades will still fail because the simultaneous-accept rule makes each responder uncertain about whether they will actually be the matched partner, and because senders must guess others’ values. Overall, the game tends toward incremental, low-risk exchanges rather than complex multi-step strategies, with Unicorn shifting his portfolio toward more RED and fewer BLUE by the end.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, everyone has symmetric chip holdings and only their own values. No one yet knows others’ willingness to trade, so proposals are likely to be simple and conservative. Players can all see that GREEN has a narrow, high public range and BLUE has a broad, low one, so trading BLUE for GREEN looks obviously attractive if feasible. Unicorn also sees internally that his RED is relatively valuable and will try to accumulate RED by offering BLUE, but he does not yet know whether Badger or Peacock will accept. Round 0 thus serves as information-gathering: small pilot offers that test others’ responsiveness, without risking large losses. Accepted trades will update beliefs that certain exchanges (e.g., BLUE for GREEN, BLUE for RED) are feasible and profitable.

After Round 0, two informative trades have occurred. Badger successfully traded BLUE for GREEN with Peacock, signaling that small, symmetric BLUE-for-GREEN swaps can be mutually profitable. Unicorn then purchased RED from Badger using BLUE, revealing a willingness to pay surprisingly generously in BLUE for RED. Badger now holds more RED and slightly fewer GREEN than initially, and Peacock holds more GREEN and fewer BLUE. Unicorn has fewer BLUE and more RED. Players now update beliefs: Badger infers that her BLUE is quite expendable and that Unicorn especially values RED; Peacock infers that trading GREEN away for BLUE may not always be wise, and that Unicorn’s preferences over RED/BLUE might be nonstandard. In Round 1, proposers are more targeted: Badger may try to offload low-value BLUE for either GREEN or possibly RED again, Peacock becomes more cautious about giving up GREEN and may instead attempt to trade his still-low-value BLUE for RED from Unicorn, and Unicorn continues to seek additional RED while trimming BLUE or even trading away some GREEN if he can cheaply gain more RED.

By Round 2, players have observed that: (1) BLUE-for-GREEN swaps are attractive but not always accepted as players protect their remaining GREEN, and (2) Unicorn is indeed eager to buy RED using BLUE, but only some offers succeed as Badger and Peacock become more aware that they are giving up relatively valuable RED. Portfolio positions have shifted: Unicorn now has substantially more RED and fewer BLUE, while Peacock and Badger have more GREEN and less BLUE, with their RED stocks partially depleted. With only one proposal remaining per player, there is little time for multi-step strategies or reputation building. Each player focuses on clear, one-shot surplus improvements. Badger tries one last low-risk trade to upgrade BLUE into GREEN or to sell a bit of RED at a good rate if available; Peacock may make a small, fairness-oriented trade but avoids large sacrifices of GREEN or RED; Unicorn, seeing the end of the game, attempts a final aggressive RED-acquisition offer using his still-low-valued BLUE. Some offers will be declined because the margins are no longer attractive enough: Badger and Peacock have already made their most profitable BLUE-to-GREEN and BLUE-to-RED trades and are less willing to part with remaining high-value chips in the final moves.
